theme: an oligarchic faction's bid to OVERTHROW a democracy by promising stability, money, and a foreign patron
problem: As the leader of the oligarchic faction in Corcyra, you have secured a secret promise of Corinthian ships and silver to overthrow the Athenian-backed democracy, but your co-conspirators are losing nerve after a botched assassination exposed the plot; you must decide how to proceed before the democratic faction arrests you all.
facets: The democratic faction already suspects a coup and is detaining known oligarchs for questioning, meaning time is measured in hours.
The Corinthian fleet is anchored three days away but will not sail unless they receive a signal confirming the city is in friendly hands and the Athenian garrison is neutralized.
Your own faction is split between hardliners who demand you strike now with the men you have and moderates who want to broker a power-sharing deal to avoid a bloodbath.

angles.directive: Storm the council house with your armed hetairoi before dawn, butcher the democratic leaders, and seize the harbor signal tower to call in the Corinthian fleet immediately, accepting a night of slaughter to guarantee total control.

Send your most trusted captain on a fast skiff to tell the Corinthians the window has closed; then disperse your men, burn the evidence, and melt back into the city while publicly blaming the plot on a rival family so the democracy exhausts itself chasing phantoms while you wait for a better season.

Approach the democratic general with an offer to betray your own hardliners in exchange for immunity and a seat on the reorganized war council, revealing the Corinthian plot to earn credibility and splitting the democratic faction between those who will pardon you and those who will try to execute you anyway.

Leak a forged letter suggesting the democrats' own treasurer embezzled funds meant for the Athenian garrison, then approach the Athenian commander as a loyal friend of order offering to 'uncover' the scandal in return for his troops backing an emergency coalition government led by you—effectively flipping the patron relationship from Corinth to Athens.

threads: I will dispatch my hetairoi to the council house before the third night watch, killing the democratic archons in their chambers, then immediately torch the harbor signal platform to flash the prearranged Corinthian recognition code.

Send your fastest cutter under a captain who knows the Corinthian seal to deliver a recall order and a planted rumor that the Aegisthus family orchestrated the botched assassination, then have your men scatter the forged correspondence in the Aegisthus warehouse before torching your own meeting hall and vanishing into the dockworkers’ tenements.

I will immediately present myself to the democratic general and offer to name every hardliner in my faction along with the exact time and signal the Corinthian fleet expects, in exchange for a sworn amnesty and a voting seat on the reorganized war council, knowing this forces the democrats into a fratricidal split between those who need my intelligence to preempt the invasion and those who will demand my execution regardless.

We fabricate a ledger documenting the democratic treasurer’s embezzlement of Athenian garrison funds, then deliver it directly to the Athenian commander with a proposal: his hoplites secure the council chamber while I publicly “uncover” the scandal, and in return, he backs an emergency coalition government under my leadership with his troops as the guarantor. This replaces the entire Corinthian dependency with a live Athenian military guarantee that forestalls arrest, splits the democratic faction over the scandal, and turns the Athenian garrison into our own enforcement arm in a single stroke.

judge.note: Strategies 3&4 assume unrealistic democratic/Athenian rationality under pressure.

theme: exploiting a sudden battlefield ADVANTAGE to force a hard PEACE now vs pressing on for total victory
problem: You are an Athenian general who has just shattered the Spartan fleet at Naxos, leaving the Spartan commander dead and his ships destroyed; you can now credibly demand a favorable peace that isolates Sparta from its allies, or you can press on to attack the Peloponnesian coast and dismantle Sparta’s entire naval capacity forever.
facets: Athens' treasury is severely depleted after years of war, and the Assembly may not fund another season of campaigning unless you bring back plunder or a treaty.
Sparta's allied Corinthian fleet is still intact at its harbor in Lechaion, and any delay allows them to regroup and join with other Peloponnesian forces.
The Athenian fleet's crews are exhausted and far from home, and a single storm or reverse could turn a triumphant expedition into a catastrophe with no rescue.

angles.directive: Exploit the shock and disorganization of the enemy to strike the Corinthian fleet at Lechaion immediately, destroying the last major Peloponnesian naval power in a single decisive blow before they can prepare a defense.

Send envoys to Corinth and other Spartan allies now, offering a separate peace that recognizes their autonomy and grants them favorable trade terms, peeling them away from Sparta and presenting Athens as the leader of a new maritime league rather than a conqueror.

Use the victory to proclaim a pan-Hellenic naval alliance against the Persian threat, inviting even Sparta to join under Athenian leadership, thereby reframing the conflict from a war between Greek states into a common defense of Hellas and making those who refuse seem unpatriotic.

Withdraw the fleet to Athens with the Spartan prisoners and spoils, let the Assembly claim the glory and negotiate from a position of demonstrated strength, while preserving the fleet as a deterrent, so that the costs and risks of any further offensive fall on the city’s democratic decision rather than your own command.

threads: Sail the intact Athenian battle line immediately to Lechaion under cover of darkness, launching a concentrated ramming and boarding assault at dawn with fire ships in the van to burn the Corinthian triremes still beached or moored, giving them no time to form a defensive line. This eliminates the last Peloponnesian fleet in a single stroke and turns their hulls and naval stores into the plunder that secures Assembly funding, rendering further campaigning unnecessary.

Dispatch Callias and a handpicked diplomatic corps to Lechaion at first light with a ratified decree offering Corinth full autonomy, exemption from tribute for five years, and privileged access to the Piraeus grain route in exchange for immediate neutrality and naval disarmament. Simultaneously, send fast triremes to Epidaurus, Troezen, and Hermione carrying identical terms, transforming Sparta’s remaining coastal allies into neutral buffer states under Athenian economic hegemony before the Spartan gerousia can convene.

Dispatch Athenian heralds to every Greek polis—including Sparta—to formally propose a permanent Pan-Hellenic naval alliance against Persia, with a unified treasury funded by proportional contributions from all members and an Athenian-appointed navarch holding operational command over the combined fleet. Simultaneously, present the decree to the Athenian Assembly as the fulfillment of the victory at Naxos, framing any refusal—especially Sparta’s—as a betrayal of Hellenic freedom that would forfeit the right to call upon Greek aid against any future barbarian invasion.

Withdraw the fleet and prisoners directly to Piraeus, then immediately request the Assembly convene an ekklesia where you present the spoils and captives as evidence of victory while formally ceding the decision on further operations to a public vote, thereby transferring the financial and strategic risk of an extended campaign onto the city’s democratic apparatus and off your command.

judge.note: Strategy 3 fantasizes Pan-Hellenic unity; Strategy 4 evades rather than decides.

theme: a small NEUTRAL city pressured to SUBMIT to a stronger power or stake survival on justice and distant allies
problem: As Athenian envoys demand Melos abandon its long neutrality, pay tribute, and join their empire against Sparta, the Melian council must choose between certain subjugation and a near-hopeless war whose outcome will shape their city's very existence.
facets: Athens offers an ultimatum with no middle ground: submit as a tributary subject or be annihilated as an example to other neutrals.
Sparta is kin by Dorian blood and the nominal protector of free Hellenes, but its fleet is weak and its willingness to risk war for a distant island remains untested.
The Melian commons, unaware of Athens' full military capacity, clamor for resistance, while the oligarchs fear both Athenian siegecraft and their own restive poor.

angles.directive: Fortify the walls, arm the populace, and openly defy Athens, slaughtering the envoys' escort to signal irrevocable resolve—forcing Sparta's hand and betting that Athenian fear of a costly, protracted siege on an island they cannot easily supply will break their will.

Send secret envoys to Syracuse and other wealthy western colonies, offering Melos as a forward naval station in exchange for immediate relief forces, while engaging the Athenians in protracted negotiations about surrender terms to buy months of precious time.

Offer Athens a voluntary alliance rather than subjection—Melos contributes ships, crew, and a harbor for patrols against Spartan privateers, retaining internal autonomy and its ancestral gods, thus giving Athens the strategic basing it wants without the expense of garrisoning a resentful conquest.

Evacuate the women, children, and elders to Crete under sacred truce, then let the remaining men of fighting age endure the siege to the last, transforming Melos into a martyr-city whose deliberate, sacrificial destruction shames Spartan honor before all Hellas and kindles lasting resistance to Athenian tyranny.

threads: We will ambush the Athenian escort inside the prytaneion during the next assembly, behead them, and send the envoys back with the heads in a single skiff to ensure Athens understands no negotiation remains possible; simultaneously, we will decree the immediate manumission and armament of all slaves and metics willing to man the walls, paying them from confiscated oligarchic estates, and will mine the cliffside approach to the harbor with pre-cut stone blocks rigged to collapse onto any landing force.

Dispatch a single trireme under cover of darkness with two trusted oligarchs bearing sealed tablets that deed Melos’s harbor to Syracuse as a permanent naval station in return for a relief squadron within ninety days, while the council publicly presents Athens with a meticulously detailed draft treaty—covering tribute schedules, garrison rights, and judicial autonomy—and insists on referring each clause back to the ekklesia for ratification, stretching the process into a months-long procedural labyrinth.

The council will table a formal symmachia—not subjection—whereby Melos immediately transfers operational control of its harbor and a standing squadron of five triremes to the Athenian nauarch for anti-privateer patrols, while retaining exclusive internal jurisdiction, its ancestral cults, and a permanent exemption from the phoros. Athens gains the strategic basing and hulls it needs to choke Spartan raiders without diverting hoplites to garrison a hostile population, and Melos converts its neutrality into a shield of reciprocal obligation that neutralizes the ultimatum’s binary.

We invoke the sacred panhellenic truce to evacuate all non-combatants—women, children, and elders—straight to the Dorian city of Gortyn in Crete under herald’s immunity, leaving only men of fighting age sworn to extinction on the island. We then seal the gates, reject negotiation, and deploy a strictly protraction-based defense that maximizes Athenian casualties per wall-breached hour, ensuring the final sack of a deliberately emptied polis registers across Hellas as an indelible indictment of Spartan honor.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume Athenian restraint implausibly; Syracuse relief fantasy.

theme: a besieged city's choice to SURRENDER on terms, hold out for a rescue that may never come, or break out by force
problem: You command Potidaea, garrisoned by a Corinthian-led force, besieged by Athens for two years with famine setting in; the Athenian general offers terms—dismantle your walls, expel the Corinthians, pay tribute—yet Spartan envoys secretly promise a relief fleet that may arrive too late or never at all, while your own hoplites grow mutinous from starvation and talk of forcing a mass breakout by night.
facets: The Athenian terms would preserve the city's population but permanently subordinate it as a tribute-paying subject, losing Corinthian alliance and autonomy.
The promised Spartan relief is militarily plausible but politically uncertain—Sparta's council vacillates, and a single storm could scatter the fleet.
Internal cohesion is crumbling: the Corinthian officers demand defiance, the citizen militia increasingly favors surrender, and the civilian population faces mass starvation within weeks.

angles.directive: Launch the breakout immediately with every able-bodied hoplite and armed citizen before starvation worsens—overwhelm a single section of the Athenian circumvallation at night, abandon the city, and march the entire population to friendly territory in Chalcidice, accepting heavy losses as the price of denying Athens both the city and its people.

Open secret negotiations with the Athenian commander to accept terms but with a hidden protocol: retain a garrison in the inner citadel, expel only the most anti-Athenian Corinthians to satisfy appearances, and offer your own son as hostage for good conduct—effectively becoming an Athenian ally rather than subject, trading formal tribute for military cooperation against other Chalcidic cities.

Exploit the mutinous mood to stage a theatrical public reconciliation: gather the assembly, announce that both surrender and breakout mean death, then propose a third path—send a delegation directly to the Athenian assembly in Athens, bypassing the general, to argue that sparing Potidaea as a free neutral would split the Corinthian-Spartan alliance more effectively than destroying it, staking everything on a rhetorical appeal to Athenian strategic calculation.

Hold out at all costs by imposing deliberate, visible rationing on the garrison first—the Corinthians eat last—to extend supplies by weeks, publicly execute the loudest mutineers, and make a ritual vow to burn the city with everyone inside if the Spartans do not arrive by the next full moon, thereby binding the garrison to a martyrdom pact that dares Athens to face a city that prefers self-immolation to surrender.

threads: Order a mass breakout tonight: concentrate every hoplite and armed civilian against the Athenian circumvallation’s southwestern salient at the drainage culvert where the palisade is lowest, breach with rams and a human wave, then immediately fire the city’s granaries and magazines to deny Athens all material benefit. Pull the entire population through the gap under cover of darkness and force-march them along the coastal track to Olynthus, accepting up to a third losses as the cost of preserving the city’s people and denying Athenian victory.

I dispatch a sealed offer via a trusted intermediary to the Athenian commander, proposing we publicly accept the terms but retain our garrison in the inner citadel under my direct command, expel only the twelve most irreconcilable Corinthian officers to satisfy appearances, and send my own son as hostage to guarantee our new compact. In exchange for this concealed autonomy, I pledge Potidaea’s remaining hoplites as an allied contingent for Athenian campaigns against the Chalcidic League, converting formal tribute into military cooperation and securing our survival as a de facto co-belligerent rather than a subject.

Convene the assembly and theatrically reframe the choice as death-by-surrender or death-by-breakout, then pivot to a third path: dispatch a handpicked citizen embassy directly to the Athenian ekklesia, bypassing the besieging general entirely. That embassy’s sole task is to convince Athens that preserving Potidaea as a demilitarized neutral will permanently fracture the Corinthian-Spartan axis—a strategic prize far greater than a starved-out ruin.

Centralize all remaining grain under armed guard, impose a visible rationing schedule where native Potidaeans eat first and the Corinthian garrison receives half-portions only after the civilians are served, and publicly behead the three loudest pro-surrender hoplites in the agora. Then bind every soul inside the walls—soldier, citizen, and slave—by a ritual oath before the temple of Hera: if no Spartan sail is sighted by the next full moon, we seal the gates, drench the city in pitch, and immolate ourselves, presenting Athens with nothing but a smoldering monument of defiance.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 3 assume unrealistic Athenian/ekklesia receptiveness; 4 risks mutiny.

theme: spending the state's last RESERVES on one decisive campaign vs husbanding them for a long war of attrition
problem: As Syracuse's general, you hold the last significant treasury of the democratic faction while Sparta's blockade tightens; you must either spend those reserves to launch a single, all-or-nothing naval breakout to cripple the enemy fleet, or conserve the funds to sustain years of defensive siege warfare.
facets: The fleet is crewed by citizen-hoplites who are also farmers; keeping them at sea too long risks harvest failure and famine, eroding the very population base needed for a long war.
Mercenary captains, unpaid for weeks, are negotiating secretly with Spartan emissaries; their loyalty dissolves unless hard silver appears within days.
The city's oligarchic exiles, now advising Sparta, have promised to deliver Syracuse intact if the democracy exhausts itself—making inaction as politically lethal as defeat.

angles.directive: Empty the treasury into a single massive fleet sortie: arm every able hull with double pay to lure mercenaries back, seek out the Spartan blockade squadron in open water, and annihilate it before harvest season, staking the regime's survival on one crushing victory.

Use the silver not for battle but to buy the loyalty of the Spartan alliance's weakest link—Corinthian naval detachments—offering their admiral a share of Syracuse's future grain tribute in exchange for their withdrawal, fracturing the blockade from within.

Convert the crisis into a constitutional rebirth: publicly melt the state's silver into a new citizen-army land-grant fund, promising every rower a plot of oligarch-confiscated land after the war, transforming the conflict from a treasury's survival into a social revolution that can weather any siege.

Hoard the reserves completely—pay the mercenaries just enough to prevent immediate mutiny but accept the blockade's slow strangulation, deliberately sacrificing external trade and political popularity to outlast Sparta's own fragile coalition, which must fracture when their allies' tribute demands go unmet.

threads: Dispatch every combat-ready quinquereme with rowers drawn from the citizen levy and offer triple hazard pay in minted silver at the docks to flip the wavering mercenary captains within the hour, then sail west to force the Spartan squadron into a dawn engagement off Cape Pachynus where their superior seamanship is neutralized by confined waters and our ramming doctrine breaks their line before they can signal the exiles’ agents inside the walls.

Dispatch the treasury's silver not to the docks but to the Corinthian admiral's tent, sealing a pact that pays him immediately in coin and guarantees him an annual tenth of Syracuse's grain yield once the blockade lifts, contingent on his squadron's prompt departure. This turn fractures Sparta's encirclement not by ram but by contractual obligation, converting a hostile fleet into a passive creditor whose withdrawal leaves the blockade geometrically untenable.

We will publicly melt the remaining treasury silver into a single, mass-issued “Land-Grant Token” for every citizen-rower, each token legally deedable against a specific, surveyed parcel of exile-oligarch estate to be seized on victory, with the fund’s physical smelting ceremony held in the agora tomorrow as a witnessed constitutional act. This transforms the rowers’ immediate wage crisis into an irreversible personal land claim that can only be honored by the democracy’s survival, making mutiny or surrender equivalent to forfeiting their post-war homestead and converting the siege into a class war the oligarchic exiles can no longer defuse by merely outwaiting our treasury.

You pay the mercenaries only the exact silver required to keep them from opening the gates tonight, then seal the treasury and let the citizen-farmers return to their fields under the cover of the blockade itself, converting Syracuse into a closed agrarian garrison that waits not for a naval victory but for the moment Corinth and Thebes default on Sparta’s tribute demands and pull the enemy coalition apart from within.

judge.note: Strategies 1&3 assume rower loyalty survives contact; 2 assumes Corinth's defection is purchasable; 4 assumes coalition fractures predictably.

theme: a CIVIL-WAR faction's temptation to invite a FOREIGN POWER inside the walls to crush its domestic rivals
problem: As leader of the aristocratic faction in a plague-ravaged Corcyra, you must decide whether to invite a Spartan garrison inside the walls to crush the popular faction before they execute their own planned massacre of your families.
facets: Spartan troops, once inside, may never leave and could reduce your faction to a client-tyranny rather than liberators.
The Athenian fleet patrols nearby waters and would view a Spartan garrison as a permanent threat to their trade route, risking immediate siege.
Your own oligarchic followers are divided between radical members who welcome any foreign sword and moderates who fear betraying the city's autonomy more than death itself.

angles.directive: Launch a preemptive night strike against the popular leaders' safe houses and armories, decapitating their coup before they strike, accepting the risk of open street combat but keeping the city free of foreign boots.

Offer the popular faction a formal power-sharing pact with hostage exchanges and rotating magistracies, deliberately exposing their extremist wing by forcing them to publicly reject a credible peace and splinter from their moderates.

Secretly evacuate your faction's families and treasury to a fortified estate in the countryside under cover of night, ceding the city temporarily to the popular faction while preserving your base to retake it when their Athenian patrons are distracted by war elsewhere.

Publicly renounce factional identity and declare a sacred truce administered by the temple of Hera, reframing the conflict not as oligarchs versus democrats but as impiety versus civic salvation, daring either side to violate sanctuary to seize power.

threads: We will launch a synchronized throat-cutting of the popular chiefs in their own beds by a handpicked cadre of thirty oligarchic veterans at the second night watch, immediately followed by seizure and barricading of the harbor arsenal to arm our loyalists before the mob stirs. This internal decapitation preempts their coup and slams the gates on Spartan intervention by delivering a pacified city to the Athenians as a fait accompli with no foreign boots on Corcyran soil.

We publicly propose a rotating dual-magistracy with exchanged children as wards, then leak forged correspondence between the popular faction’s extremists and Athenian agents to isolate them when they reflexively denounce the offer as aristocratic entrapment.

We relocate the families and treasury through the postern gate at the third hour, using the sewer channel beneath the eastern wall to mask movement from both the popular faction’s watchers and our own radical oligarchs, while leaving a skeleton household staff to maintain lit braziers and normal foot traffic until dawn.

I publicly dissolve the aristocratic faction and declare all its members under the protection of the Temple of Hera, reframing any armed mobilization as an act of impiety rather than political rivalry. By moving our families into the sanctuary and empowering the priests to administer oaths of truce, I force the popular faction to either violate sacred ground and alienate every pious citizen, or accept a ceasefire that dissolves their pretext for massacre.

judge.note: Strategies 1&3 assume flawless execution; 4 underestimates priest leverage.

theme: an able general operating FAR FROM HOME, with discretion to liberate or coerce cities beyond his orders' intent
problem: As an Athenian general operating in Thrace far from Athens, you have just recaptured the rebellious colony of Amphipolis; your standing orders are to secure the city and return hostages, but you learn that executing the pro-Spartan oligarchs will provoke open revolt across the region, while releasing them strengthens Sparta’s influence and dishonors Athenian blood spilled to take the city.
facets: Your troops expect vengeance and material reward after a hard siege; denying them risks mutiny or a collapse in authority hundreds of miles from reinforcement.
Local Thracian tribes and neighboring cities are watching closely—your treatment of Amphipolis will determine whether they side with Athens, Sparta, or remain neutral.
Athens itself is politically fractured; whatever you do, your enemies in the Assembly will use your decision to prosecute you upon return, while your allies will need a defensible narrative.

angles.directive: Execute the oligarchic leaders publicly, install a democratic garrison, and dare the region to revolt—crush any uprising swiftly to demonstrate that rebellion against Athens carries an annihilatory price.

Spare the oligarchs and publicly install them in a new joint council with Athenian-allied democrats, granting them honors and a stake in the city’s governance to convert them from Spartan clients into Athenian partners.

Send the prominent prisoners back to Athens for trial under the Assembly’s authority, you taking no local irrevocable action yourself—your hands remain clean while the burden of blood or clemency falls on the distant demos.

Proclaim Amphipolis a free and autonomous city under Athenian protection, abolish the tribute obligation, and offer it a mutual defense pact—reframing the rebellion not as treason to be punished but as a misunderstanding among equals now corrected by a magnanimous new settlement.

threads: Publicly behead the pro-Spartan oligarchs in the agora before the assembled population, then install a garrison of Athenian marines under a handpicked democratic officer authorized to execute any citizen who voices dissent. When the neighboring tribes revolt, I will march out immediately, burn their villages to ash, and exterminate every male of fighting age so that the region learns rebellion against Athens means annihilation.

I will re-charter Amphipolis under a new constitution that merges the surviving oligarchs and local democrats into a single probouleutic council with equal voting shares, then publicly bestow Athenian proxeny and a golden crown on each oligarch who takes the oath, transforming them into personally invested stakeholders whose survival depends on Athens’s success rather than Spartan restoration.

I embark the Amphipolitan oligarchs immediately on the state trireme *Paralos* under armed marine escort with a sealed formal indictment requesting the Assembly adjudicate their treason; simultaneously, I issue a public proclamation that Athenian law reserves capital judgment for the demos alone, converting the army’s demand for vengeance into a distant civic spectacle while I remain visibly bound by standing orders to secure the city and return hostages.

You will immediately dispatch a herald to proclaim Amphipolis a free and autonomous polis, permanently abolishing all Athenian tribute obligations and extending a formal mutual defense pact, thereby converting the city from a subject tributary into a nominally equal allied protectorate whose garrisons are now stationed by "invitation" rather than conquest.

judge.note: Strategy 4 unrealistic; Strategy 1 fantasy-escalation; others grounded.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: The Athenian Assembly must decide the punishment of Mytilene, a crucial naval ally that has just been recaptured after a dangerous revolt; the choice will signal to every other subject-city whether rebellion invites annihilation or a more measured response.
facets: The empire's other subject-cities are watching, and excessive cruelty may harden resistance in future revolts, while clemency may be seen as weakness.
The Mytilenean oligarchs who instigated the revolt have already been surrendered; the common people are largely ambivalent, offering an opportunity to split the population.
Athenian naval dominance hinges on tribute and alliance; destroying Mytilene removes a potential naval base, but keeping it intact requires garrison costs and risk of re-revolt.

angles.directive: Execute all adult males, enslave the women and children, and demolish the city’s fortifications to serve as a stark deterrent.
Transport the captive population to Athens and hold them indefinitely as hostages, leaving a small garrison to administer the city while deferring any final sentence.
Spare the population, execute only the ringleaders, install a democratic regime, and station a permanent garrison to bind Mytilene as a loyal tribute-paying ally.

Pardon the entire city and announce a new imperial charter offering equal trade rights and citizenship pathways to all allies who remain loyal, transforming the empire into a league of mutual benefit.

threads: To split the population is to leave the empire's spine exposed; instead, execute every Mytilenean male of arms-bearing age, enslave the rest, and raze the city’s walls and docks to the waterline so that the empty site becomes a navigational warning rather than a port.

The Athenian fleet will immediately embark the entire Mytilenean demos—excluding the surrendered ringleaders—and transport them to Athens for indefinite custodial detention as living collateral against the island’s future compliance. A skeleton garrison of two hundred hoplites will remain to administer the city and extract tribute, while the Assembly formally suspends judgment, converting the population into a permanent, inert bargaining chip rather than a corpse or a free ally.

Identify and summarily execute the 30 surrendered oligarchic ringleaders, then immediately convene a Mytilenean assembly to ratify a democratic charter and elect a pro-Athenian council under the supervision of an Athenian garrison commander.  
Station a permanent garrison of 500 hoplites and a trireme squadron in the harbor, funded by a fixed annual tribute reassessed at 15 talents, thereby converting Mytilene from a rebellious naval ally into a securely garrisoned democratic tributary.

The Assembly immediately issues a universal pardon to Mytilene and proclaims a new Imperial Charter granting full trade reciprocity and a five-year military service path to Athenian citizenship for any allied city that pays its tribute without interruption. The charter is funded by converting a fixed percentage of tribute into a league-managed naval infrastructure fund, with voting seats on its allocation board allocated proportionally to contribution levels, thereby transforming the coercive empire into an opt-in mutual defense and commercial league.

judge.note: Strategy 4 fantasizes opt-in empire; others lack reaction chains.

theme: whether to EXECUTE or RANSOM prisoners of war whose treatment will shape how every future enemy surrenders
problem: As an Athenian strategos, you hold the leaders of a recaptured rebellious ally as prisoners; your treatment of them will determine whether future defecting cities surrender quickly to preserve their people or fight to the last man, shaping the empire's stability.
facets: The prisoners are the city's oligarchs and best generals—executing them would cripple the rebellion's leadership, but ransoming them could provide urgent silver for the overstretched fleet.
Multiple wavering allied cities are watching this episode closely; Sparta is actively courting them, and their own oligarchic factions may exploit any perceived Athenian cruelty or weakness.
The Athenian assembly is deeply divided and will retrospectively judge your decision; a misstep could see you charged with bribery or incompetence, ending your command and your life.

angles.directive: Execute the prisoners publicly as a brutal lesson: revolt means annihilation, compelling fear-driven loyalty and ensuring future rebels either submit instantly or fight to the death without hope of negotiation.
Ransom the captives for a high sum immediately, using the funds to strengthen your fleet; the ambiguous signal buys time and resources while leaving the strategic message about surrenders unresolved.

Spare the prisoners and co-opt them into a new, tighter alliance structure, using their survival as leverage to transform the hostile city into a closely bound and dependent ally under permanent Athenian oversight.

Release them unconditionally with a public proclamation of Athenian clemency, reframing the empire from a coercive tyranny to a league of consent, aiming to dissolve the incentive for desperate resistance in all future contests.

threads: We will execute the captured oligarchs and generals at dawn in the city’s agora, then impale their severed heads on the main gates as a permanent monument to Athenian retribution. We will simultaneously issue a decree to all allied cities stating that any future revolt will trigger the identical eradication of its leadership without exception, forcing every oligarchic faction to choose between immediate capitulation or total annihilation with no space for negotiation.

I privately demand 200 talents from the oligarchs’ estates through their families, payable within three days to the fleet’s treasury at Mounychia, and upon receipt I release the captives at dusk with a sealed amnesty that explicitly defers any judgment of their rebellion to the Athenian demos; the silver funds immediate hull repairs and oarsmen’s wages, while the ambiguous release signals neither mercy nor terror, denying Sparta a propagandist’s wedge.

We install the surviving oligarchs as a perpetual Athenian-appointed council under a revised treaty that replaces their independent military command with a permanent Athenian garrison and mandatory tribute calibrated to their remaining wealth, then publicly broadcast their signatures on the new charter to every wavering ally as proof that submission yields survival and restored local status rather than annihilation.

I order the immediate, unconditional release of all captured oligarchs and generals, escorted under truce to their city gates with a herald proclaiming that Athens neither annexes nor punishes, but invites their return as autonomous partners in a consensual Delian League, the terms of alliance now void of tribute compulsion. Simultaneously, I dispatch Athenian envoys to every wavering allied city carrying the same decree, publicly reframing the empire as a voluntary compact so that any future oligarchic faction urging resistance must openly argue against a demonstrable offer of unforced cooperation, exposing their rebellion as self-serving rather than defensive.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes voluntary reframing works; ignores Spartan counter-narrative risk.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: Following the suppression of Mytilene’s revolt, the Athenian Assembly must determine the city's fate to deter other tribute-paying allies from rebellion, knowing that the fleet holds the city but allied resentment is already hardening across the empire.
facets: The demos and the rowers demand swift, spectacular revenge after a costly siege, threatening domestic stability if denied.
Other restive allies are watching closely; excessive cruelty may provoke desperate resistance rather than obedience.
Mytilene’s harbor and fleet represented a major asset to the league, whose destruction or transformation carries long-term economic and strategic consequences.

angles.directive: Annihilate Mytilene: execute all adult males, enslave women and children, raze its walls, and leave the site desolate as an unforgettable warning to all subjects.

Spare the populace, try and execute only the ringleaders, impose a democratic constitution and a permanent garrison, and integrate Mytilene’s fleet into the League with favored terms to bind its interests to Athens.
Summon a tribunal of allied cities to judge Mytilene’s guilt and pronounce sentence, sharing responsibility and making the League complicit in whatever follows.

Dissolve Mytilene as an independent polis and refound it as an Athenian cleruchy, expropriating land for Athenian settlers and erasing the rebellious identity by making it a permanent extension of Attica.

threads: Annihilate every Mytilenean male of arms-bearing age, transport the women and children to the Athenian slave markets to offset the siege’s cost, demolish the harbor fortifications, and scuttle their triremes so that the empty anchorage becomes a permanent visual scar on the Aegean trade routes.

We will disembark the ringleaders identified by the returning exiles, try them on the spot, and execute them publicly, while the demos is immediately convened to ratify a new democratic constitution under the protection of a permanent Athenian garrison. Simultaneously, we will absorb the Mytilenean triremes into the League fleet but grant their crews a five-year exemption from tribute payments, converting their naval capacity into a privileged instrument of Athenian power that makes defection costlier than loyalty.

To execute this, Athens convenes a formal judicial panel composed of one voting delegate from each tributary ally, tasking them with reviewing the evidence of Mytilene’s breach of treaty and issuing a binding collective sentence that the Athenian fleet then mechanically enforces.

The Assembly should decree the abolition of Mytilene as a corporate entity and carve its territory into 3,000 hereditary land allotments assigned by lot to Athenian cleruchs, who will garrison the harbor and operate its trireme sheds as a permanent naval station under an Athenian governor. This surgically replaces a restive tributary with a piece of Attica that pays no tribute but generates fleet capacity and a landed surveillance class, turning a liability into an imperial organ.

judge.note: Strategies 1,2,4 lack realistic downstream modeling; Strategy 3 underspecified.

theme: whether to honor a costly DEFENSIVE ALLIANCE with a quarrelsome partner that risks dragging the state into a wider war
problem: Mytilene, a key Athenian ally with a powerful fleet, has revolted and seeks Spartan protection; honoring the alliance requires Athens to commit its strained navy to a protracted siege across the Aegean while a Peloponnesian fleet could strike Attica or intercept the expedition, yet abandoning Mytilene signals to all subject cities that rebellion carries no cost.
facets: The Athenian treasury is dangerously depleted after years of war and plague, making a prolonged naval expedition a potential existential financial strain.
Sparta has promised Mytilene support, meaning Athens risks a decisive naval battle far from home while its own coast is exposed.
Other tribute-paying allies are watching closely; perceived leniency could trigger a cascade of revolts, but excessive brutality will fuel lasting hatred.

angles.directive: Send the fleet immediately to blockade Mytilene, capture its ships, and execute the ruling oligarchs before Spartan reinforcements arrive, demonstrating that revolt is suicidal and decapitating the rebellion at its source.

Announce acceptance of Mytilene's secession and withdraw the demand for tribute, publicly reframing the alliance as voluntary, thereby conserving Athens' depleted strength and denying Sparta a pretext for immediate intervention.

Secretly approach the Mytilenean demos—who were armed by their oligarchs only to suspect their loyalty—and offer Athenian backing for a democratic coup in exchange for renewed allegiance, splitting the city from within without a single Athenian trireme engaging the Mytilenean fleet.

Deliberately delay the response while withdrawing the tribute fleet from the Lesbos station, allowing the Peloponnesians to commit their forces to Mytilene's aid, then sacrifice Attica to a temporary invasion while the concentrated Athenian fleet ambushes the Spartan navy in a decisive battle of annihilation.

threads:
Dispatch the Athenian fleet under Thrasyllus to Mytilene with orders to bypass negotiation, seal the harbor before dawn, and storm the oligarchic council chamber—executing every identified ringleader on the spot and impounding their triremes for immediate integration into our line.  
Strip the city of its naval capacity and ruling class in a single surgical strike that leaves no leadership alive to negotiate with Sparta and demonstrates that rebellion terminates not in terms but in extinction.

The Athenian assembly votes to dissolve Mytilene’s tributary obligations and publicly reconstitute the Delian League as a voluntary defensive compact, issuing a decree that recognizes Mytilene’s independence while simultaneously recalling the expeditionary fleet to Piraeus before it commits to a siege.

Dispatch a covert envoy to the Mytilenean demos—the very men the oligarchs armed and now distrust—offering Athenian backing for a democratic seizure of the acropolis and the fleet in return for renewed allegiance. This turns the revolt into an internal coup that neutralizes the Mytilenean navy without a single Athenian trireme engaging, and signals to every subject city that rebellion’s cost is not annihilation but regime change.

Recall the tribute fleet from its Lesbos station immediately, stripping the siege to draw the Peloponnesian navy into committing its full force to Mytilene’s relief. Abandon Attica to a season of ravaging while the concentrated Athenian fleet intercepts that expeditionary force in the open Aegean and annihilates Sparta’s seaborne capacity in a single decisive battle.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume unrealistic Spartan passivity; 2 collapses alliance credibility.

theme: whether to keep a fragile, mistrusted PEACE TREATY or resume the war while the advantage still holds
problem: You are Clearidas, Spartan commander at Amphipolis, ordered by the ephors to hand the city over to Athenian envoys under the fragile Peace of Nicias; but you have fresh intelligence that the Athenians are actively subverting your Thracian allies, and your scouts report an Athenian grain convoy sailing nearly undefended. You must decide before the envoys arrive: keep the mistrusted peace, or resume war while your local advantage is sharpest.
facets: The peace treaty is already fraying: Corinth and Boeotia refuse to sign, and Spartan commissioners suspect Athens will exploit any handover to rearm.
Your Thracian allies see any compliance as betrayal and threaten to switch allegiance to Athens or go independent, costing you the whole region.
A window of vulnerability exists: the Athenian fleet is temporarily reduced, but your own garrison is just enough for a quick operation—if you delay, the balance will tip against you.

angles.directive: Launch a surprise attack on the grain convoy and seize the Athenian envoys before they reach the city; present Sparta with a victorious fait accompli that makes the peace irrelevant and cripples Athens’s capacity to strike back.

Stall the handover by sending a messenger to Sparta claiming local unrest makes immediate transfer impossible, while you secretly fortify Amphipolis, call up nearby allies, and delay the envoys with ceremonial hospitality and endless clarifications.

Summon the wavering Thracian chieftains and formalize a permanent defensive alliance under Spartan protection, offering them joint garrisons and a shared council; this transforms the region into a block that Athens cannot attack without openly breaking the peace.

Propose to the Athenians that Amphipolis become a neutral free city under a Panhellenic guardianship—invite Corinth, Thebes, and Argos to send overseers, reframing the dispute as a sacred truce of all Hellenes that neutralizes the flashpoint without surrender.

threads: Destroy the Athenian grain convoy with our garrison’s peltasts and a picked lochos boarding fishing vessels under cover of night, then intercept and imprison the envoys on the Eion road before they sight Amphipolis.

I send a fast rider to Sparta with a sealed dispatch citing a sudden Thracian uprising that makes immediate handover impossible, then quietly summon the Edonian chieftains to a war council while my adjutant drowns the Athenian envoys in ceremonial banquets and endless procedural clarifications.

Summon the Thracian chieftains immediately and formalize a permanent defensive pact by granting them joint garrison commands inside Amphipolis and a shared council with Spartan veto power, thereby making any Athenian move against the region an unambiguous breach of the peace that compels Spartan retaliation.

I will deliver the envoys a sealed proposal—not a refusal—that transfers Amphipolis into a Panhellenic board of overseers drawn equally from Athens, Sparta, Corinth, Thebes, and Argos, declaring the city inviolable neutral ground under a shared sacred truce, thereby making any Athenian military move against it an act of sacrilege witnessed by their own allies.

judge.note: Strategies 3–4 assume unrealistic allied compliance; no failure modes.

theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of ALLIANCE AND REINFORCEMENT that may be a bluff or a trap
problem: Nicostratus, besieging a rebellious Aegean city with dwindling supplies and winter approaching, receives a charismatic envoy from the Thracian king Sitalces promising a large army in exchange for gold and future alliance; the envoy's offer could be genuine relief or a treacherous plot to join the city's defenders and destroy the Athenian forces.
facets: The envoy, bearing a small token force, is silver-tongued and offers immediate joint action against the rebels, but his king is known for breaking oaths.
Winter storms threaten Athenian supply lines, and the besieged city may soon receive Spartan aid, making the siege untenable without reinforcement.
A captive rebel under torture claims Sitalces has secretly pledged to aid the city, though the information may be fabricated to sow discord.

angles.directive: Demand a hostage of royal blood and order the Thracian army to approach in small, disarmed companies that can be easily cut down at the first sign of treachery—accept the alliance only if the envoy submits to these fatal proofs of loyalty.

Keep the envoy feasted and negotiating while a swift trireme slips away to the Thracian capital to observe military preparations; avoid any commitment until true intentions can be inferred, risking the envoy's patience.

Welcome the Thracian reinforcement but disperse its warriors among Athenian units as skirmishers and labor, separating them from their leaders and using them to absorb the hardest fighting in the assault on the city.

Propose that the Thracian army strike a different rebel stronghold far from the siege, turning the offer into a diversion that aids the Athenian campaign while keeping the ambiguous ally at a safe distance and testing their intent in a less critical theater.

threads: Nicostratus will seize the envoy’s token force as surety and demand the envoy immediately produce a son of Sitalces as a blood hostage, while issuing a non-negotiable ultimatum that the Thracian army disarm, fracture into twenty-man packets, and advance along separate, narrow ravines where Athenian peltasts and archers are already concealed with orders to massacre any group that deviates from its assigned path or retains a blade.

Nicostratus detains the Thracian envoy with lavish banquets and deliberately circular negotiations while a swift trireme, crewed by handpicked oarsmen and a trusted phrourarch, departs under the moonless sky to observe Sitalces’ mobilization yards and harbor readiness.  
He signs nothing, pledges nothing, and feeds the envoy only wine and delay until the reconnaissance vessel returns with evidence of Thracian intent, accepting the calculated risk that the silver-tongued diplomat’s patience collapses into overt threat.

Accept the envoy’s offer with public honors, then immediately fragment the Thracian force: assign every warrior to an Athenian lochos as a psilos or laborer under the direct command of Athenian file-leaders, while isolating their chieftains in a ceremonial “council of war” watched by a picked guard. At dawn, order these integrated Thracians to form the first assault wave against the city’s postern gate, using their bodies to exhaust the defenders’ missiles and reveal weak points before the hoplites advance.

Nicostratus accepts the alliance but redirects the Thracian envoy to immediately assault the rebel-held fortress of Amphipolis, sixty miles inland, with his token force serving as the vanguard under the guns of an Athenian squadron stationed offshore to prevent collusion. Any hesitation or deviation from the assault plan triggers the execution of the envoy’s hostage retinue and the sinking of Thracian transports, converting the offer into a diversion that either delivers a strategic blow to rebel supply lines or exposes treachery safely outside the siege perimeter.

judge.note: Strategies 3&4 assume Thracian compliance under duress; unrealistic downstream.

theme: a small NEUTRAL city pressured to SUBMIT to a stronger power or stake survival on justice and distant allies
problem: A small neutral coastal city, Abdera, confronts an Athenian fleet threatening siege at dawn unless it submits to Athenian hegemony, pays tribute, and abandons its independence. The city’s council knows Sparta might intervene but only after the sacred Karneia festival ends, leaving them a single night to decide their fate.
facets: Athenian military force sits offshore, overwhelming and immediate, making resistance suicidal without aid.
Spartan assistance is possible but ritually delayed and uncertain, dependent on distant decisions and a religious calendar.
Abdera’s walls are strong but its grain stores are low, and no ally is close enough to lift a siege before starvation.

angles.directive: Refuse the ultimatum, man the walls, and dispatch a fast trireme to Sparta, staking everything on Spartan honor arriving before the city falls.
Request a three-day sacred truce to consult the city’s gods, while secretly sending envoys to Sparta and Thracian mercenaries, buying time for distant help to materialize.
Offer to join the Delian League as a voluntary ally providing warships and a naval station rather than tribute, negotiating privileged autonomy within the Athenian order.

Proclaim Abdera a sanctuary of Apollo, dispatch a delegation to Delphi to seek sacred truce, and invite the Athenian commander to arbitration before the Amphictyonic Council, reframing the demand as a violation of divine law.

threads:
The council orders the immediate midnight launch of the city’s sole fast trireme, crewed by the polemarch and a handpicked team of freeborn rowers sworn to silence, to row nonstop hugging the unlit coastline and evade the Athenian blockade by using the fishing channels, carrying a blood-sealed copy of the Spartan mutual-defense pact for direct delivery to the ephors.  
Simultaneously, every able-bodied citizen and slave is conscripted to strip the agora and inner quarters of all timber, hide, and iron to construct counter-siege mantlets and fire-pots, while the seaward gate is permanently barricaded with rubble and the granary placed under armed guard with a strict half-ration decree enforced at spearpoint.

The council sends a herald at dawn to request a three-day sacred truce from the Athenian commander, formally invoking the necessity of consulting the city’s patron deity and framing any refusal as a direct act of impiety that would stain the siege. Simultaneously, under cover of the truce negotiations, two hand-picked envoys exit through the seaward drainage culvert—one bearing a coded message to the Spartan ephors confirming the exact Karneia end date and offering a year’s tribute in exchange for immediate post-festival mobilization, the other carrying a chest of silver to hire a Thracian hill tribe to strike the Athenian foraging parties from the north within seventy-two hours.

Abdera’s emissary sails out before dawn to offer Athens not tribute but a ratified treaty of voluntary alliance, pledging five triremes and the immediate transfer of the harbor’s eastern quay as a permanent Athenian naval station in exchange for a charter that exempts Abdera from the phoros assessment and reserves internal legislation to its own council.

The council passes an emergency decree consecrating Abdera to Apollo, and a swift trireme departs at once carrying a theōros to Delphi to petition for a sacred truce and mandatory Amphictyonic arbitration. Simultaneously, a herald is dispatched to the Athenian fleet with a formal summons for the commander to submit his demand to the Amphictyonic Council, framing the threatened siege as a sacrilege against the god’s sanctuary under panhellenic nomos.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 assume unrealistic panhellenic/Spartan timing constraints.

theme: a CIVIL-WAR faction's temptation to invite a FOREIGN POWER inside the walls to crush its domestic rivals
problem: As the leader of the oligarchic faction in Corcyra, you can seize permanent control of the city by opening the gates to the Spartan fleet anchored offshore, but doing so means inviting a foreign garrison that may never leave and will make your city a permanent battlefield in the wider war.
facets: The Spartan admiral has privately demanded that you execute your own democratic family members as proof of loyalty before his marines set foot inside the walls.
The democratic faction currently holds the council chamber and the grain stores, meaning any siege without Spartan help would starve your own supporters before the enemy.
Neutral envoys from Syracuse have arrived offering to mediate, but accepting their terms would expose your recent massacre of democratic prisoners to public judgment and likely execution.

angles.directive: Accept the Spartan terms, execute the demanded hostages, and open the gates — then, once the democrats are crushed, immediately fortify the acropolis against your new allies, stockpile provisions, and prepare to betray the Spartans the moment the Athenian relief fleet appears on the horizon.

Stall the Spartan admiral with false promises while publicly signaling distress to Athens — dispatch a fast trireme to beg for immediate intervention, portraying yourself as a victim of Spartan coercion, and buy time by faking negotiations with the democrats until the Athenian sails are sighted.

Seize the Syracusan mediation offer and reframe it publicly — approach the democratic leaders with a proposal for a joint 'Corcyran Liberty Council' that excludes both Sparta and Athens, offering to share power and pardon all past crimes on condition that both factions unite to fortify the city and declare armed neutrality against all foreign fleets.

Refuse both foreign powers entirely and instead stage a dramatic public trial of your own faction's worst offenders for the earlier massacres, sacrificing a handful of your own men to reclaim the moral legitimacy of the oligarchic cause, then rally the city around a new constitution that positions Corcyra not as client but as mediator and arbitrator for all neutral Greek cities seeking to end the wider war.

threads: Execute the democratic hostages at dawn to satisfy the Spartan admiral’s proof, then open the sea gates and let his marines storm the council chamber while we simultaneously pull our loyalists back to the acropolis with every sack of grain. When the Athenian relief fleet breaks the horizon, we ignite the prearranged signal fire on the temple roof, sally out to torch the Spartan ships moored in the harbor, and cut down the garrison commander in the chaos before his men can regroup.

Dispatch my swiftest trireme under cover of darkness to Piraeus bearing a sealed plea that frames the Spartan ultimatum as a gun to my head and pledges to hold the city as a Trojan horse for Athenian intervention, while my herald tells the Spartan admiral I require seventy-two hours to orchestrate the family executions discreetly and suggests he retreat his squadron over the horizon to avoid provoking a premature democratic uprising.

I publicly convene the democratic leaders under the Syracusan envoys’ observation and propose a Corcyran Liberty Council that splits the archonships evenly, grants retroactive immunity for all intra-factional killings, and unites both militias under a single command tasked with sealing the harbor and mounting a citywide defense against any external fleet. I then inform the Spartan admiral that his demand for familial executions is rendered void by this unified regime, making his garrison unnecessary and defining any attempt to force entry as an act of war against a non-aligned Corcyra.

I will immediately arrest the seven oligarchs who personally ordered and oversaw the prisoner massacre, try them before the assembled citizen body tomorrow with their own victims’ families as formal prosecutors, and execute them by sunset under the old homicide law to publicly sever the faction from its crimes. At the same moment, I will propose a new constitution establishing Corcyra as an inviolably neutral custodian of a standing Panhellenic arbitration council, open to all unaligned cities, thereby transforming our internal purge into the founding act of a third bloc that renders both Spartan garrison and Athenian counterattack strategically irrelevant.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume unrealistic coordination; 3 underestimates Spartan resolve.

theme: an oligarchic faction's bid to OVERTHROW a democracy by promising stability, money, and a foreign patron
problem: You lead the oligarchic faction in Mytilene, preparing to revolt against Athenian-backed democracy; the Spartan admiral Alcidas offers ships and gold but demands you massacre all democrats immediately upon seizing power, while a moderate democrat proposes a unity government that would preserve the city but leave your faction permanently vulnerable.
facets: Alcidas's fleet is within range but famously unreliable; if he withdraws before the coup, your faction will be exposed and crushed.
The democratic faction controls the grain distribution and city walls; any bloodshed will destroy the economic infrastructure Sparta's gold is meant to stabilize.
A growing middle group of merchants fears both Athenian reprisal and Spartan domination, and their allegiance will decide whether any government can hold the city.

angles.directive: Accept Alcidas's terms, massacre the democratic leadership, and present the fait accompli to the city as an irreversible break from Athens, gambling that terror and Spartan backing will cow the merchant middle into submission.

Pretend to accept the unity government while secretly using Spartan gold to buy off key democratic sub-factions and the merchant class, building a shadow majority that will vote democracy out of existence legally, then quietly exile rather than kill your opponents.

Reject both the massacre demand and the unity government; instead propose a new 'Council of the Worthy' limited-franchise constitution that excludes the landless poor but enfranchises the merchant middle, then invite Spartan and Athenian observers to guarantee it, transforming Mytilene into a neutral experiment neither hegemon can attack without breaking faith.

Stall the coup entirely; send Alcidas home with thanks but no commitment, then secretly approach Athens with intelligence about Spartan intentions in exchange for a revised treaty that grants Mytilene greater autonomy and your faction explicit immunity from future prosecution, deferring regime change until Athenian power weakens.

threads: We arm the dockworkers’ guild with Spartan gold and Alcidas’s marine detachment, timing the strike to coincide with the grain distribution so that we seize both the warehouses and the roster of ration recipients in a single hour, then execute the democratic council inside the grain hall itself while the merchant ships are physically blockaded in the harbor by Alcidas’s triremes.

I will publicly accept the unity government while secretly channeling Alcidas’s gold to purchase the grain-distribution clerks and the merchant guild’s assembly blocs, assembling a legal supermajority to dissolve the democratic offices and replace them with a timocratic council.  
Once the vote is secured, I will offer the democratic leaders the choice of exile under Spartan safe-conduct, leaving the walls, granaries, and trade networks intact.

I will convene the thirty wealthiest merchants and the non-massacre wing of my own faction to ratify a property threshold excluding the landless poor but enfranchising all who own a workshop or ship, then immediately dispatch envoys to Alcidas and the Athenian commander declaring Mytilene a neutral “Council of the Worthy” with both powers invited to send observers whose presence makes any attack a violation of a publicly sworn truce.

We dispatch a trusted envoy to Alcidas with a lavish gift and a formal resolution thanking him for his offer while declaring the moment "not yet ripe," then immediately send the same envoy onward to the Athenian strategos with a detailed dossier on Spartan fleet movements and Alcidas’s promises, proposing a revised treaty that explicitly immunizes our faction from prosecution and shifts Mytilene’s tribute to a fixed, nominal sum in exchange for our ongoing intelligence on Spartan operations in the Aegean.

judge.note: Strategies 1,3 ignore Athenian response; Strategy 4 assumes implausible Spartan passivity.

theme: launching a distant, glittering FOREIGN EXPEDITION that overextends the state vs consolidating strength at home
problem: You are Nicias, co-commander of the Athenian armada sent to conquer Sicily—a glittering prize that promises wealth and dominion, but you know it risks fatal overextension while a hostile Sparta and resentful allies wait at home. The assembly has ordered the reduction of Syracuse; how do you steer this expedition?
facets: The expedition’s sheer size and distance drain Athenian treasury and manpower, leaving Attica exposed to Spartan invasion.
Your co-commander Alcibiades and a suspicious democracy will interpret any retreat or delay as cowardice or treason, stripping you of authority.
Sicilian cities are deeply divided: some, like Segesta, may welcome Athens, but powerful Syracuse is prepared for a long and costly war with uncertain local support.

angles.directive: Strike Syracuse with overwhelming force before winter, storming its walls or provoking a decisive hoplite battle to end resistance at a blow.
Conduct a show of force along the coast, demand tribute from weaker cities, then declare the goal accomplished and return the fleet home under pretext of unfavorable omens or supply shortages.

Offer a genuine alliance to anti-Syracusan factions, promising autonomy and Athenian protection to build a ring of friendly cities that contain Syracuse without bleeding Athens into a war of conquest.

Redirect the expedition to the Peloponnese, seizing a permanent fortified base like Pylos to strangle Sparta’s supply lines, turning the venture from a western gamble into a direct blow at the enemy’s heart.

threads: We land the entire expeditionary force on the beach beneath Epipolae under cover of darkness, storm the unfortified heights before dawn, and roll down the slope at first light to shatter Syracuse's hastily assembled hoplite line in a single envelopment. This eliminates the need for siegeworks and breaks their will before winter, turning the city’s discord into capitulation.

We sail the entire armada in a tight coastal procession past Segesta, Selinus, and the smaller Greek poleis, extracting formal pledges of tribute and neutrality under threat of immediate siege, while conspicuously avoiding any direct engagement with Syracuse. Once the tribute contracts are sealed and loaded, I order the fleet to turn back at Cape Pachynus, citing the unfavourable flight of the sacred eagles during departure and the sudden spoilage of grain stores as divine and logistical grounds for terminating the expedition before winter.

I will immediately detach a fast trireme to Leontini and the Sicel interior with envoys authorized to ratify permanent mutual-defense pacts that recognize their full internal autonomy and install a small Athenian naval garrison at Catana as a forward shield, not a base of conquest. By binding Segesta, Leontini, and the Sicel chieftains into a formal league that encircles Syracuse and denies it allies, I transform the expedition from a siege army into the muscle behind a diplomatic encirclement that compels Syracuse to negotiate on Athenian terms or face slow strangulation without a single assault on its walls.

I will countermand the Sicilian course and divert the full expeditionary force to the headland of Pylos, where we will immediately disembark the hoplite corps to erect a permanent double-walled fortress commanding the narrows of Sphacteria, thereby severing Sparta’s overland route to Messenia and its helot-labor base. Simultaneously, I will detach a squadron to raid the Laconian coast and incite a mass helot revolt, transforming the armada from a distant gamble into a direct, existential siege of Sparta’s economic heart.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 hallucinate unrealistic escape routes; foresight collapses.

theme: exploiting a sudden battlefield ADVANTAGE to force a hard PEACE now vs pressing on for total victory
problem: You are Brasidas, Spartan commander in Thrace. After seizing Amphipolis, you hold a decisive advantage: Athens offers peace ceding key cities, but you could instead push south to Eïon and the mines, risking winter and Athenian reinforcement, seeking to break Athenian power in the north completely.
facets: Your army is battle-hardened but weary; supplies are limited and winter is coming.
Athenian envoys offer terms that would detach several wavering allies, but they may be stalling for time to send reinforcements by sea.
Your local allies, who expect plunder and autonomy, might turn against you if you impose a harsh peace or continue a grinding campaign.

angles.directive: March immediately on Eïon and the Athenian colonies, storming their fortifications before winter locks the passes, and sweep Athenian influence from the region forever.

Entrench at Amphipolis, secure the walls and granaries, and wait; let winter and logistics bleed the Athenians, forcing them to make the next move and revealing whether their peace offer is genuine or a stall.

Offer the captured cities autonomous alliance under Spartan protection, asking only contributions for common defense; convert them into willing bulwarks against Athens that deprive the enemy of allies without costly sieges.

Proclaim a pan-Hellenic congress of all northern cities to settle borders and autonomies under a permanent common peace, using your victory to threaten Athens while inviting Persian mediation, thus recasting the war as a defense of Hellenic freedom.

threads: We march on Eïon at speed, using the cover of the first winter storm to scale the walls with ladders before the garrison can reinforce, while our Thracian allies torch the ship sheds and block the harbor with debris. Once Eïon falls, we dispatch a flying column to burn the mining settlements and salt the works, forcing every Athenian colonist to flee or die before the passes close.

Brasidas orders the immediate reinforcement of Amphipolis’s landward walls and the seizure of all grain stores within a day’s march, while publicly announcing that the army will winter in place and receive no envoys until spring; this converts the campaign into a static siege of position, forcing Athens to either escalate costly naval supply runs through winter storms or reveal their offer as a delay by sailing south themselves.

I will immediately release Amphipolis and the other captured cities as self-governing allies under Spartan protection, requiring only a fixed annual contribution of troops and silver for the common defense, while garrisoning key passes with mixed Spartan-local forces to block Athenian land access without advancing further.

Brasidas immediately issues a formal convocation to all northern cities—including Athens and the Persian satrap—to a pan-Hellenic congress at Amphipolis, where a permanent common peace will be drafted under Spartan guarantee, with borders and autonomies fixed by binding arbitration. He simultaneously halts all offensive operations and publicly declares that any refusal to attend will be treated as a declaration of war against the collective freedom of Hellas, thus transforming his military momentum into a diplomatic ultimatum that forces Athens to either submit to a multilateral settlement or appear as the aggressor before Persian mediators.

judge.note: Strategy 4 fantasizes diplomatic leverage Brasidas lacked; Persian mediation implausible.

theme: an able general operating FAR FROM HOME, with discretion to liberate or coerce cities beyond his orders' intent
problem: Brasidas, operating deep in Thrace with ambiguous orders to detach Athenian allies, must decide how to bring the divided city of Torone into the Spartan fold without alienating neighboring cities or provoking a protracted siege his small force cannot sustain.
facets: Torone's internal politics are split between a pro-Athenian democratic faction and a pro-Spartan oligarchic clique, each fearing the other's ascendancy.
Brasidas is far from Spartan reinforcements and his campaign's momentum depends on whether cities perceive him as a liberator or a brutal conqueror.
His standing orders are to weaken Athens by freeing its subjects, but the ephors at home mistrust overly independent commanders and expect tangible results without excessive garrisons.

angles.directive: Storm Torone by night with the pro-Spartan faction's help, execute the democratic leaders, and install a garrison—turning the city into an unmistakable example that neutrality will not be tolerated.

Bypass Torone entirely, marching past it to sever more vital Athenian supply lines; let the city stew in its indecision until the shifting balance of power compels it to seek Spartan terms on its own.

Open secret talks with the moderate pro-Athenian leader, offering him the archonship of a 'free' Torone under Spartan protection if he delivers the city peacefully—co-opting him into a power-sharing arrangement that neutralizes the democrats.

Proclaim before the assembled citizens that he comes not as Sparta's agent but as the liberator of all Hellenes from Athenian empire, inviting them to expel both Athenian and Spartan partisans and join a new league of genuinely autonomous cities.

threads:
We stage a night assault with the oligarchs opening the gates, then systematically round up and publicly execute every democratic leader on the spot to extinguish resistance at its root.  
We install a permanent garrison under Spartan command, transforming Torone into a stark ultimatum for all wavering cities: abandon Athens or face annihilation of your pro-Athenian class.

Brasidas will execute a forced night-march past Torone’s walls, refusing all contact, and drive directly for Eion to seize its grain stores and blockade the Strymon estuary. This stranglehold on the region’s supply artery leaves Torone’s factions isolated and starving for terms only a Spartan guarantee of food shipments can now satisfy.

Brasidas sends a covert envoy to the leader of Torone’s moderate democratic faction with a precise offer: the archonship of a formally autonomous Torone under Spartan protection, contingent on him engineering the city’s peaceful surrender. The arrangement binds him to form a governing council that merges his own adherents with a handful of pliable oligarchs, structurally excluding both the radical pro-Athenian democrats and the extremist pro-Spartan clique from power.

Brasidas convenes the Toronean assembly and declares the immediate dissolution of both the pro-Athenian democratic council and the pro-Spartan oligarchic clique, replacing them with a provisional synoikismos of all free Toroneans sworn to a charter of autonomy guaranteed by neither Athens nor Sparta but by a mutual-defense pact with Brasidas’s army alone.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 assume unrealistic downstream compliance; Strategy 3 underestimates radical faction backlash.

theme: whether to keep a fragile, mistrusted PEACE TREATY or resume the war while the advantage still holds
problem: You are Nicias, commander in Athens. The fragile Peace of Nicias has held for three years but mutual mistrust festers. Suddenly, a clandestine faction in neutral Megara offers to betray the city to you tonight, giving Athens a permanent garrison on the isthmus—yet acting means open war with Sparta, who still holds treaty allies. Do you keep the mistrusted peace, or seize the fleeting advantage while Sparta is distracted?
facets: The advantage is fleeting: Sparta's army is currently entangled in a border dispute but will soon be free.
Honor and divine witness: the oaths sworn before the gods bind Athens not to attack neutrals; breaking them invites impiety and loss of confidence among allies.
Deception risk: the Megarian faction could be a Spartan ruse to make Athens the treaty-breaker, which would rally the Peloponnesians under Sparta.

angles.directive: Seize Megara at once. Break the treaty openly, strike now while Sparta is unprepared, and accept the war, trusting that the strategic gain will outweigh the loss of face.
Stall the Megarians with promises, but do nothing tonight. Send spies to verify their loyalty and buy time, hoping to preserve the peace while secretly reinforcing the border for a future move.

Foster the Megarian democrats' uprising with money and arms, then recognize their new regime as independent and offer an alliance—claiming the city's own internal change makes it not a neutral party attacked by Athens, but a willing partner, thus avoiding a technical breach.

Publicly declare that Sparta's recent treaty violations (such as garrisoning disputed territory) have already voided the peace. March on Megara openly, framing the action as a just preemptive defense, not an aggression, and force Sparta to either back down or admit the war is of their own making.

threads:
Send the entire Athenian expeditionary force through the Megarian gates the moment the faction opens them, and publicly announce the annexation at dawn as a divinely sanctioned necessity, not a breach.  
Garrison the isthmus passes with a permanent fort and recall the fleet to blockade the Corinthian Gulf, forcing Sparta to fight on terrain of our choosing while their army is still in the border dispute.

Send two trusted proxenoi to Megara tonight with sealed tokens promising Athenian silver and citizenship to the faction leaders upon verified delivery of the lower gate, while simultaneously instructing our garrison at Eleusis to begin camouflaged reinforcement of the border forts without mobilizing the full levy.

We smuggle funds and a cadre of Athenian engineers to the Megarian democratic faction tonight, enabling them to seize the acropolis and proclaim a new regime by dawn; at first light, we publicly recognize the revolution as an internally driven change and extend an immediate defensive alliance, treating the city not as a conquered neutral but as a willing partner whose invitation renders our garrison entry lawful.

I will issue a herald’s proclamation before the Assembly that Sparta’s garrison in disputed border territory constitutes a material breach voiding the Peace of Nicias, and then march the full levy openly to Megara at dusk, announcing we accept their request for protection against inevitable Spartan reprisal—thus transforming a covert betrayal into a lawful, preemptive defense of the isthmus. Sparta must then either stand down and tacitly concede our right to garrison Megara under the logic of their own violation, or mobilize and bear the onus of reigniting the war they already broke.

judge.note: Strategies 1,3,4 assume Sparta won't retaliate decisively; underestimate coordination risk.

theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of ALLIANCE AND REINFORCEMENT that may be a bluff or a trap
problem: As the democratic leader of Corcyra holding the city after a vicious civil war, you receive a charismatic exiled oligarchic envoy promising that if you allow the oligarchs to return peacefully and share power, they will bring a Corinthian fleet already at sea to defend against an imminent Athenian punitive expedition—this salvation might be genuine, or a trap to gain entry and slaughter your faction.
facets: The envoy's eloquence and apparent sincerity make the promise compelling but impossible to verify before the gates are opened.
Your exhausted city lacks the strength to fight both the returning oligarchs and an Athenian assault, making outside reinforcement desperately needed.
The oligarchic faction has a known history of oath-breaking and brutal reprisals, yet their fear of Athenian destruction might now force genuine cooperation.

angles.directive: Arrest the envoy and execute him publicly as a spy, using his death to harden your faction’s resolve to fight alone and deter any internal conspiracy.
Keep the envoy in comfortable but closely watched seclusion, stalling for time while secretly dispatching your own fast ship to scout for the Corinthian fleet before giving any answer.

Accept the offer but insist the oligarchs first prove their good faith by sending their promised fleet captains ashore unarmed to help repair the city’s fortifications, merging their fate with yours under your supervision.

Secretly inform the approaching Athenians of the envoy’s proposal, offering to act as a double agent; this transforms the negotiation into a means to bait the oligarchs and Corinth into a premature engagement on your terms.

threads: Seize the envoy mid-address, charge him with espionage under wartime decree, and execute him publicly at the main gate within the hour. His displayed corpse will cauterize all internal wavering, forcing every citizen into irreversible commitment and preempting any oligarchic fifth column from coordinating with the fleet.
