I will place the envoy in the inner guest chambers under constant armed watch, framing it as honored protection while explaining that the Assembly must deliberate; simultaneously, I will hand-pick a crew for the *Aetos*, our fastest trireme, with sealed orders to sail north immediately, scout the approaches to Corcyra, and return with a confirmed sighting—or absence—of the Corinthian fleet before any gates are unbarred.

"I condition the oligarchs’ return on the immediate, unarmed disembarkation of their Corinthian fleet captains to join work gangs repairing the Long Walls under direct loyalist militia supervision, merging their personal survival with the city’s physical defenses. This move transforms their naval expertise into a directly controlled defensive asset while the unarmed vulnerability functions as a non-negotiable litmus test—compliance yields both reinforcement and hostages, refusal exposes the trap without ever opening a gate."

Dispatch a swift, unmarked skiff under cover of night to the Athenian fleet with a sealed offer to become their intelligence asset, feeding them the exact timing and signal for the oligarchs’ re-entry so the Athenians can intercept the Corinthian squadron at the harbor mouth. This converts the negotiation from a binary open-or-shut decision into a controlled anvil-and-hammer operation, where the city gates open only to spring a predetermined Athenian trap on both the returning oligarchs and their Corinthian protectors.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Athenian cooperation without plausible mechanism; risks catastrophic miscalculation.

theme: whether to honor a costly DEFENSIVE ALLIANCE with a quarrelsome partner that risks dragging the state into a wider war
problem: Athens must decide whether to honor a defensive alliance with Corcyra, whose quarrel with Corinth risks dragging Athens into open war with the Peloponnesian League. Sending ships to Corcyra's aid may shatter the Thirty Years' Peace; refusing may permanently cede the balance of naval power to Sparta's allies.
facets: The alliance is explicitly defensive: if Corcyra is deemed the initial aggressor, Athens has no treaty obligation and can legally abstain.
Corcyra possesses the third-largest fleet in Hellas; its defeat would deliver those ships to Corinth and tilt the strategic naval balance decisively against Athens.
Corinth is an essential member of the Spartan alliance; any direct Athenian action against Corinthian forces risks activating the full Peloponnesian League under Spartan leadership.

angles.directive: Deploy the full Athenian fleet to fight alongside Corcyra, aiming to annihilate Corinth's naval capacity in a single decisive engagement and eliminate the long-term threat, regardless of immediate repercussions with Sparta.

Send a token contingent of ten triremes under strict orders to observe but not engage unless Athenian vessels are directly attacked, thus upholding the alliance's form while deferring any real military commitment and avoiding provocation.

Open secret negotiations with Corinth, offering Athenian backing for Corinthian commercial interests in the West in exchange for Corinth abandoning the expedition against Corcyra, thereby converting a rival into a dependent partner and neutralizing the clash.

Propose binding third-party arbitration under the terms of the Thirty Years' Peace, reframing the military confrontation as a legal dispute and compelling Sparta to restrain Corinth or risk being seen as the violator of Panhellenic order.

threads: Athens deploys its entire 300-trireme fleet to rendezvous with the Corcyraean navy at the Sybota islands and annihilate the Corinthian battle line in a single, overwhelming combined engagement, deliberately sinking or capturing every vessel to permanently erase Corinth’s naval capability. By transforming the legal defensive commitment into a unilateral offensive annihilation, we eliminate the strategic threat before Sparta can mobilize, regardless of the Thirty Years’ Peace.

Dispatch ten Athenian triremes to Corcyraean waters with sealed orders to anchor beyond engagement range, observe all maneuvers, and advance only to recover Athenian sailors or hulls directly struck; if no such attack occurs, they record the outcome as neutral witnesses and return, satisfying the defensive pact’s letter without crossing Sparta’s red line.

Instruct the envoy to open an unacknowledged backchannel with Corinth’s oligarchs offering publicly deniable but operationally concrete Athenian naval convoy guarantees for Corinthian grain shipments to Sicily and preferential access to Piraeus emporium fees, conditioned solely on Corinth recalling its expeditionary force and consenting to binding Athenian arbitration of the Corcyra matter. This transfigures Corinth from Spartan spearhead into a commercially dependent client severed from the dispute, dissolving the clash at its source without mobilizing a single trireme.

Athens must formally invoke the binding arbitration clause of the Thirty Years' Peace, delivering a precise ultimatum to Sparta: either Corinth submits the Epidamnian dispute to a neutral tribunal of Sparta's own choosing within thirty days, or Athens will register Sparta's failure to restrain its ally as a material breach of the treaty, activating the legal machinery of the peace before a single trireme engages.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 fantasy-overreach; 2 & 3 underspecify Spartan/Corinthian reactions.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: The Athenian assembly must determine the fate of Mytilene, a subject-city that revolted, killed Athenian captives, and surrendered on promise of a fair hearing; now the assembly debates whether to annihilate the city as a warning to other restless allies or to uphold the general's pledge, knowing that leniency might embolden further revolts.
facets: The strategic logic of deterrence: a swift, brutal punishment could terrify other allies into submission, but destroying a major city costs tribute and manpower.
The binding force of the general's surrender terms: breaking the promise undermines Athenian honor and future negotiations, yet honouring it risks appearing weak.
The volatile mood of the democratic assembly, whipped by demagogues like Cleon, who frame clemency as sentimental folly that endangers the empire.

angles.directive: Vote to kill every adult male Mytilenean, enslave the women and children, and raze the city walls and harbour, leaving the site desolate as a permanent deterrent.

Spare the entire city unconditionally, reaffirm the surrender terms, restore the democratic faction, and reduce the annual tribute slightly to convert Mytilene into a loyal demonstration of Athenian magnanimity.

Settle 1,000 Athenian cleruchs on confiscated lands of the ringleaders, turning Mytilene into a military colony while leaving the rest of the population free but disarmed, thereby permanently altering its identity and preventing future rebellion.

Hand over the city to a pro-Athenian exile faction from Lesbos, empowering them to execute the oligarchic rebels and govern as a client regime under Athenian protection, so that the bloodshed is done by local hands and Athens avoids direct moral and political stain.

threads: The assembly executes every adult male Mytilenean, sells the women and children into slavery, and dismantles the city’s walls and harbour, leaving the site a depopulated ruin to function as a permanent, visible deterrent. This move converts the city itself into a negative monument—erasing its strategic utility and broadcast capacity while preserving the empire’s manpower pool through the enslaved survivors.

The assembly will issue an immediate decree annulling the prior death sentence, formally renewing the surrender pact, installing the returned Mytilenean democrats in power, and lowering the annual tribute from the original assessment by ten percent. This converts Mytilene from a crushed enemy into a permanently indebted client whose visible prosperity under Athenian mercy discredits rebellion as futile and strips future revolts of any moral cover.

The assembly executes the 1,000 identified ringleaders, confiscates their estates, and settles 1,000 Athenian cleruchs on that land as a permanently armed garrison-farming class, while disarming the remaining Mytileneans but leaving them free and tribute-paying. The Mytilenean fleet is seized and absorbed into the Athenian navy, the city walls are razed, and the cleruchy’s presence transforms the polis into a demilitarized agricultural dependency from which rebellion is physically impossible.

Recall the pro-Athenian Lesbian exiles, place the surrendered city under their legal jurisdiction, and have them publicly convict and execute the oligarchic rebels under Mytilenean law.  
Athens then formalizes their regime as a tribute-paying client state under garrison protection, transforming the retribution into a localized purge that leaves no Athenian hand directly bloodied.

judge.note: Strategy 2's 'visible prosperity' claim underspecified; Strategy 4's exile-regime stability unexamined.

theme: spending the state's last RESERVES on one decisive campaign vs husbanding them for a long war of attrition
problem: The ruling oligarchs of Mytilene, facing the tightening Athenian siege, must decide whether to spend the city's last fleet and grain reserves on a single breakout battle or to husband them for a protracted defence while waiting on Sparta's promised relief.
facets: Sparta's fleet is repeatedly delayed, leaving Mytilene isolated behind Athenian naval cordons.
The demos grows restive and mutinous as grain rations shrink, threatening to open the gates to the enemy.
A successful breakout could shatter the siege and link with Peloponnesian forces, but failure would mean immediate annihilation before relief can arrive.

angles.directive: Mass every ship and hoplite for a dawn sortie to smash through the Athenian blockade; stake the city's fate on one pitched sea battle to destroy the besieging squadron.
Seal the harbours, impose severe rationing, and dispatch fast messengers to prod Sparta; let the siege drag on and hope that time, Athenian fatigue, or a Peloponnesian fleet breaks the blockade.

Send heralds to the Athenian commander offering to surrender the revolt, resume tribute, and hand over hostages in return for lenient terms, using the remaining treasury to negotiate a soft return to the empire.

Divert the reserves to bribe other subject cities and islanders to rise in rebellion simultaneously, transforming Mytilene’s single siege into an empire-wide conflagration that forces Athens to divide its fleet.

threads: We will launch every trireme and embark every armed hoplite at false dawn, forming a dense ram-headed wedge aimed at the Athenian flagship’s station to shatter their cordon in a single shock assault; simultaneously, we burn the remaining grain stores on the quay and seal the land gates from within, forcing the demos to choose between the oar and the sea.

Dispatch the Krypteia-like harbor-masters to physically seal every quay with chains and scuttled hulks while reducing the demos to a single barley cake per head per day, then send the swiftest trireme to Sparta under cover of storm to deliver an ultimatum that Mytilene’s capitulation will hand Athens a fortified base for ravaging Laconia.

Send heralds to the Athenian commander Paches offering immediate surrender of the revolt, resumption of imperial tribute from the city treasury, and delivery of the oligarchic ringleaders as hostages, in exchange for ungarrisoned autonomy and the restoration of grain shipments.

The oligarchs will strip the treasury, load the last grain reserves onto the fastest triremes, and dispatch them under false pretences to Chios, the Cyclades, and the coastal demes of Lesbos itself, where trusted agents will distribute the hoard directly to local anti-Athenian factions to purchase simultaneous armed uprisings.  
This converts Athens’ single naval cordon into a dispersed, multi-front crisis that compels the enemy fleet to fragment, lifting the siege not by battle but by structural dissolution of the empire’s hold.

judge.note: Strategy 4 fantasizes empire-wide uprising; 2 oversells Spartan leverage.

theme: whether to honor a costly DEFENSIVE ALLIANCE with a quarrelsome partner that risks dragging the state into a wider war
problem: Corinth demands that Syracusan forces honor their sworn defense pact by reinforcing Potidaea against Athens, a commitment that would strip Syracuse of its best ships and invite Carthaginian opportunism at home.
facets: The treaty with Corinth is unambiguous in requiring mutual defense, but Potidaea's revolt was Corinth's instigation, not an unprovoked attack — making the obligation's trigger contestable.

Syracuse's strategic horizon is bimodal: losing naval superiority in the Sicilian straits could embolden Carthage immediately, while refusing Corinth could unravel the network of Dorian allies that buffers Syracusan ambitions.

Athens has not directly threatened Syracuse and might even welcome a pretext to stay neutral in the west if Syracuse demurs, yet abandoning Corinth now could leave Syracuse isolated when Athens looks westward after subduing Potidaea.

angles.directive: Dispatch the promised fleet but use it to strike not just Athens's expeditionary force but also seize the Athenian supply depot at nearby Sane, aiming to shatter Athenian projection in the north so decisively that Carthage loses the window to act before the fleet returns.

Make elaborate preparations and repeatedly delay sailing with diplomatic excuses—negotiate with Carthage a temporary safe-conduct, then assess whether Athens exhausts itself at Potidaea before your ships ever leave the harbor.

Offer Athens a secret neutrality-for-neutrality accord: Syracuse stays out of Potidaea in exchange for Athenian recognition of Syracusan primacy in Sicily, effectively converting the Corinthian alliance into a bargaining chip that secures your western flank.

Refuse Corinth outright and accept the dishonor and diplomatic rupture, absorbing the loss of mainland Dorian goodwill while concentrating power at home, gambling that Corinth's desperate need for allies will force them back to the table once Potidaea falls.

threads: "Syracuse will dispatch its fleet not to reinforce Potidaea but to execute a rapid strike on the Athenian supply depot at Sane, enveloping and destroying the expeditionary force before it can consolidate. This shatters Athens's northern projection so swiftly that Carthage has no operational window to exploit, transforming the treaty obligation into a preclusive elimination of both the immediate and the strategic threat."

We will publicly announce the fleet’s solemn mustering and send envoys to Carthage to secure a temporary, face-saving non-aggression pact by offering restricted trade access to Syracuse’s market, while simultaneously invoking an ancient oracle requiring a purification rite that “detains” the ships for the exact duration of the Potidaean revolt’s peak spending. Our commitment thus becomes a throttled valve: we sail only if Athenian losses reach a pre-calculated threshold—fifty percent of their deployed triremes confirmed lost or beached—a condition we quietly wire to our consuls in Corinth as divine constraint, never as choice.

I will secretly approach the Athenian assembly via a trusted intermediary with a quid pro quo: Syracuse will not send its fleet to Potidaea and will publicly classify the conflict as an internal Corinthian instigation, in exchange for Athens’ formal recognition of Syracuse’s exclusive hegemony over all Sicilian Greek cities and a binding non-interference clause covering the entire western theater. This converts the Corinthian defense pact from a strategic obligation into a traded asset, buying a guaranteed western flank calm that freezes Carthaginian opportunism and repositions the Dorian bloc as a neutral buffer rather than a chain of liabilities.

Syracuse formally voids the defense pact by declaring Corinth’s instigation of Potidaea’s revolt a material breach of the mutual-defense trigger, then immediately redeploys the entire battle fleet to permanent patrol of the Sicilian straits. We absorb the dishonor now, letting Corinth’s inevitable defeat at Potidaea dissolve their bargaining pride so that a desperate, humbled Corinth must later petition Syracuse for renewed alliance on our terms.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 3 assume unrealistic Athenian/Carthaginian passivity; oracle gambit implausibly precise.

theme: a besieged city's choice to SURRENDER on terms, hold out for a rescue that may never come, or break out by force
problem: After revolting from Athens to join Sparta, the city of Scione is now besieged by an Athenian army and blockaded by sea; Spartan relief is promised but delayed indefinitely. The Scionaean council must decide the city’s fate before food runs out, knowing that no choice guarantees survival.
facets: The Athenian blockade completely cuts off resupply; starvation is weeks away and morale is crumbling.
The promised Spartan relief force under Brasidas has been stalled by political rivalries among Sparta’s allies, making its arrival uncertain.
The Athenian commander has demanded unconditional surrender and threatens to execute all adult males and enslave the women and children, making capitulation potentially catastrophic.

angles.directive: Arm every able body, set fire to the siege engines, and launch a mass night assault on the weakest point of the Athenian siege lines to force a breakout before strength fails.

Secretly send envoys to the Athenian general offering to hand over the revolt’s ringleaders, pay immediate reparations, and pledge Scione’s remaining warships to the Delian League in exchange for clemency and re-entry as a subordinate ally.

Lock the gates, strictly ration all grain, shelter the non-combatants in the cisterns, and man the walls around the clock—hold out resolutely until the Spartan sails are sighted or famine forces the final choice.

Dispatch a desperate delegation to the neighboring King of Macedon bearing a crown and a deed of vassalage, begging him to claim Scione as his protectorate and use his diplomatic and military weight to compel Athens to lift the siege.

threads: We conscript every able-bodied male and female above the age of twelve into a single assault echelon, arm them with oil-soaked rags, pitch, and farm implements, and at the deep night hour when the Athenian watch changes we ignite their siege towers and circumvallation with a massed rush against the waterlogged eastern redoubt where their line meets the marsh. The entire civilian population follows the breach column on foot, abandoning the city, while a rearguard of elders and the wounded sets fire to the granaries and cisterns behind us to deny the Athenians any usable capture and to force their pursuit into a burning, emptied husk.

We dispatch a covert envoy under cover of night to the Athenian commander with a precise offer: the council will identify and surrender the seven known instigators of the revolt, transfer one hundred talents of silver from the temple treasury as immediate reparations, and sail Scione’s five remaining triremes into the Athenian blockade to be inducted into the Delian League, in return for a sworn treaty re-admitting Scione as a tribute-paying ally with full immunity for the populace. The council simultaneously isolates the ringleaders on the pretext of an emergency war council, securing them for transfer before any faction can mobilize resistance or alert Spartan sympathizers.

The council seals the granaries and decrees a strict daily grain ration of one cup per fighting-age male only, while relocating all women, children, and elderly into the cisterns to reduce exposed body mass and preserve water. The gates are barricaded with rubble, every able-bodied citizen is assigned a four-hour rotating watch on the walls, and any voice advocating capitulation is silenced by immediate execution to prevent the collapse of collective endurance.

The council will dispatch a secret delegation to King Perdiccas of Macedon bearing the city’s crown and a ratified deed of vassalage, formally ceding Scione as a permanent tributary protectorate in exchange for his immediate military interposition to break the siege and his diplomatic ultimatum to Athens framing further attack as an act of war against Macedon. This converts the binary choice between annihilation and surrender into a third-party territorial claim that forces Athens to weigh the cost of a wider conflict against a city already stripped of independent sovereignty.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume unrealistic Spartan/Macedonian responses; 3 ignores morale collapse.

theme: a small NEUTRAL city pressured to SUBMIT to a stronger power or stake survival on justice and distant allies
problem: The small island city of Mende, long autonomous, is confronted by an Athenian fleet demanding immediate submission to the Delian League or face siege and annihilation. Spartan envoys privately encourage resistance and promise aid, but past Spartan interventions have been slow or absent. The ruling council must decide: stake survival on distant allies and the justice of independence, or bow to superior force.
facets: Credibility of Spartan aid: past betrayals of neutral cities cast doubt on whether distant allies will actually fight for Mende.
Internal division: a powerful merchant faction favors accepting Athenian terms for trade stability, while landowners and traditionalists demand resistance, threatening civil strife.
Symbolic precedent: Mende's choice will embolden either Athenian coercion of neutrals or spark wider defiance, altering the strategic landscape of the region.

angles.directive: Declare open alliance with Sparta, fortify the city, and prepare for siege. Dispatch urgent envoys to summon the Peloponnesian army, framing resistance as a sacred defense of Greek liberty that will either force Sparta to honor its word or make Mende a martyr that shames the Spartans into action.

Initiate protracted negotiations with the Athenian commander, offering a token gesture of goodwill while secretly exploring alternative counterbalances—Corinthian mediation, Persian satrapal protection, or a pan-Chalcidian neutrality pact. Stall until winter storms render a siege unsustainable, buying months to forge a broader anti-Athenian coalition without committing to immediate battle.

Accept Athenian suzerainty but negotiate a charter that secures reduced tribute, guaranteed internal self-rule, and special privileges in Athenian grain trade in exchange for Mende’s skilled sailors and strategic harbor becoming a voluntary asset. Transform submission into a partnership that preserves de facto autonomy and economic advantage within the empire.

Proclaim Mende a sacred inviolable sanctuary dedicated to Poseidon, hosting games and pan-Hellenic rites at a new temple. Send heralds to all Greek states, including Athens and Sparta, demanding recognition of its ritual neutrality. Redefine the crisis from a power struggle to a test of piety—forcing Athens to either accept the new status or risk sacrilege and religious censure that could unravel its own alliances.

threads: We openly declare a defensive alliance with Sparta, immediately begin reinforcing the city walls and stockpiling supplies for a protracted siege, and dispatch an envoy to the Spartan ephors and the Peloponnesian League assembly, carrying a formal proclamation that frames Mende’s stand as the sacred test of Spartan vows—every day Sparta delays becomes a visible betrayal that dissolves its alliances. This forces Athens to either divert forces into a costly siege while Spartan credibility is publicly weighed, or to back down and allow Mende’s defiance to serve as the precedent for neutral cities.

The ruling council sends an envoy to the Athenian fleet bearing a ceremonial tribute and a request to formalize terms, while secretly dispatching emissaries to Corinth, the nearest Persian satrap, and the Chalcidian League to negotiate a conditional mutual-defense pact that activates only upon an Athenian attack. Every Athenian demand is met with meticulous procedural counter-questions and token concessions, dragging the exchange into winter storms that render a naval siege unsustainable and buying months to assemble a coalition that forces Athens to choose between withdrawal and a wider war.

The council sends a herald to the Athenian commander offering immediate surrender conditional on a ratified *symmachia* charter that fixes annual tribute at one-third the assessed rate, bars Athenian garrison and magistrates, and grants Mende’s grain fleet permanent right of first lading in Piraeus, in exchange for Mende’s full naval squadron and harbor operating under Athenian operational command but Mendean flag. This converts capitulation into a contractual asset transfer, locking de facto sovereignty and trade primacy inside the imperial framework as the price of voluntary naval integration.

The Council will immediately dispatch heralds to every major Greek city-state announcing Mende’s consecration as the Pan-Hellenic Sanctuary of Poseidon Aegaeus, funded by a mandatory levy on all merchant cargoes passing through its harbor, and will invite Athens, Sparta, and all other states to send delegates to the inaugural Isthmian-style games within sixty days, thereby converting the Athenian ultimatum into a binary choice between accepting Mende’s ritual neutrality or committing sacrilege that would fracture the religious legitimacy underpinning Athens’s own Delian League hegemony.

judge.note: Strategies 3–4 assume unrealistic Athenian/Greek responses; fantasy-chain overreach.

theme: a besieged city's choice to SURRENDER on terms, hold out for a rescue that may never come, or break out by force
problem: Cleonymus, the Spartan harmost besieged in the rebel city of Scione with dwindling supplies, must choose a course before the Athenian circumvallation starves his garrison and the promised Peloponnesian fleet arrives too late—or not at all.
facets: Food stocks will last only three more weeks under strict rationing; beyond that, the garrison and populace face starvation.
The Spartan fleet under Alcamenes is rumored to be assembling at Mende, but its arrival is uncertain and may be delayed by weather or Athenian patrols.
The Athenians have offered to accept surrender, sparing the garrison’s lives but enslaving the Scionaeans and handing the city to Athens—a dishonour for a Spartan officer.

angles.directive: Launch a sudden night assault on the weakest section of the Athenian siege wall, using shock and Spartan discipline to punch a corridor through which the garrison can escape and then link up with friendly forces in the countryside.

Abandon the city by stealth: have the garrison and those civilians who can keep pace slip away via a hidden channel through the marshes to the coast, boarding small boats under cover of darkness to reach neutral territory and avoid a direct fight.

Open negotiations with the Athenian commander to surrender the city on the most tolerable terms obtainable—securing safe passage for the Spartan garrison and possibly preserving some local rights, even if it means yielding the city into Athenian hands.

Resolve to hold out at all costs: tighten rations, send desperate envoys to the Spartan ephors for immediate relief, and accept that the garrison may starve or die fighting in a final stand to uphold Sparta’s reputation for unyielding endurance.

threads: We sortie at 0300 against the Athenian circumvallation’s northwestern splice—where torch-observation confirms their post is manned by Argive auxiliaries rather than hoplites—and punch a column through with the *enomotiai* in wedge, carrying only weapons and one day’s iron rations, leaving the Scionaean population in place to mask the break until dawn.

Cleonymus will evacuate the garrison and selected civilians under cover of darkness via the submerged marsh causeway known only to local fishermen, moving in single file with muffled oars to a pre-positioned flotilla of caïques hidden in the creek mouth, then steer due west to the neutral Thracian coast before dawn. He leaves behind all siege stores and surplus arms to simulate continued occupation with dummy sentries and lit braziers, ensuring the Athenians do not detect the exfiltration until the city is empty and the garrison has dissolved into the coastal marshes beyond their reach.

We send an unarmed herald to the Athenian lines at night to propose an immediate parley, offering to hand over Scione’s gates and arms in exchange for a written guarantee of safe, unmolested withdrawal for the entire Spartan garrison and a ratified clause preserving the Scionaean assembly’s right to petition Athens for local self-governance rather than mass enslavement.

Cleonymus reduces the garrison’s daily grain ration to a half-cup of barley per man and dispatches his strongest helot runner through the Athenian patrols at the darkest hour of the new moon, carrying a sealed demand for immediate naval relief to the ephors with the warning that no second envoy will follow. The remaining stores are strictly allotted to a twenty-day countdown, after which the entire force will strip the camp of non-combatants, burn the supplies, and execute a pre-dawn massed breakout toward Mende, discarding all wounded to slow Athenian pursuit and ensure the unit dies on its feet in Spartan formation.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 assume unrealistic Athenian blindness; causeway/breakout logistics unvetted.

theme: a CIVIL-WAR faction's temptation to invite a FOREIGN POWER inside the walls to crush its domestic rivals
problem: You lead the democratic faction in Megara, conspiring to open the gates to Demosthenes’ Athenian army tonight to crush the Spartan-backed oligarchs; but you have just learned the Spartan garrison is alert and moderate oligarchs hint they will share power if you renounce foreign intervention.
facets: The Athenian promise of support: Athenian hoplites are numerous and seasoned, but their entry could turn into a sack of the city, discrediting you as a puppet.
The moderate oligarchs’ overture: they offer a genuine share of the archonship and council seats, yet their trust is uncertain and hardline oligarchs may resist.
The Spartan garrison alertness: if the Athenians encounter prepared resistance, street fighting may destroy the commercial harbor, alienating the neutral merchants you need.

angles.directive: Open the gates, signal the Athenians, and fight through the streets alongside them to slaughter the oligarchs and Spartan garrison before they can mount a deadly defense.
Flee Megara with your core followers under cover of darkness, abandon the coup to avoid a doomed battle, and seek refuge in Athens to preserve the democratic cause for another day.
Accept the moderate oligarchs’ hint, open secret talks, and forge a power-sharing council that merges both factions, sending the Athenians a false signal to withdraw.

Denounce the oligarchs’ tyranny before the Megarian demos without calling in Athenians, ignite a mass uprising of the people, and expel the oligarchs on your own terms without foreign soldiers inside the walls.

threads: We drug the Spartan garrison’s evening wine with aconite through the cooperator in the mess kitchen, then at the first watch change we slit the sentries’ throats at the Postern Gate, flash the mirror signal into the olive grove, and funnel the Athenian assault column straight down the Street of the Armorers to the oligarchs’ estate before the main barracks can be roused.

We evacuate the democratic faction’s leadership cadre through the unguarded eastern postern at midnight, taking the treasury and membership rolls along the dry ravine to Athenian territory, thereby removing the only force that could legitimize either a sack or a power-sharing deal. The city is left to the oligarchs and the Spartan garrison to bleed each other without our blood on the cobbles, while we reconstitute in exile as the legitimate Megarian demos-in-waiting.

I will dispatch a trusted intermediary to the moderate oligarchs tonight with a concrete proposal for an immediate joint archonship of three from each faction and a council merged by lot, contingent on their public repudiation of Spartan interference. Simultaneously, I will send a forged deserter to the Athenian vanguard bearing a sealed message that the plot is compromised, the garrison has tripled, and the signal fire will not be lit, ensuring they stand down without approaching the walls.

By publicly indicting the oligarchs before the ekklesia for treasonous collusion with Sparta while withholding the Athenian signal, you convert latent democratic resentment into an immediate armed mob that storms the council house before the garrison can redeploy from the walls.

judge.note: Strategies 1&4 assume implausible garrison passivity; 2 abandons leverage; 3 relies on forger credibility.

theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of ALLIANCE AND REINFORCEMENT that may be a bluff or a trap
problem: As the Athenian commander besieging Potidaea, you receive an envoy from the nearby Bottiaeans offering to betray their city and join their forces with yours if you detach a third of your troops to support their coup—but the envoy’s story shifts under questioning, and his promised gates may open onto an ambush. Do you gamble on this alliance to speed the city’s fall, or reject the offer and maintain your current slow strangulation?
facets: Dividing your army not only weakens the siege but creates a vulnerable column that Bottiaean cavalry could destroy in the broken ground between cities.
If genuine, the Bottiaean alliance would immediately force Potidaea’s surrender and free your troops for the wider Chalcidice campaign, earning you acclaim in Athens.
The envoy is a known demagogue whose faction has contacts with Corinth; he could sincerely desire Bottiaean defection or be a decoy preparing a Corinthian relief force.

angles.directive: Seize the envoy and torture him publicly outside the walls for the true plot; launch a night assault on Bottiaea before any trap can be sprung.
Refuse the offer, tighten the siege cordon, and send a fast trireme to Athens requesting reinforcements and instructions to neutralize any larger Corinthian scheme.
Accept the envoy’s terms but insist his faction first deliver a hostage—his own son—and open a minor gate for a small Athenian vanguard to test the ground before committing the full detachment.

Leak word to Potidaea that Bottiaea is about to betray them to Athens; offer Potidaea generous terms to surrender and join you in a punitive strike against the double-dealing Bottiaeans, converting an enemy into an ally against the would-be traitors.

threads: Seize the envoy at sword-point before the assembly, apply the rack beneath Potidaea’s walls until he confesses the coup as a Corinthian decoy, then march on Bottiaea by night with two-thirds of the army, burning the betrayer’s gates before they spring.

I refuse the Bottiaean offer, order the immediate completion of a double circumvallation with a fortified camp facing outward, and send the fast trireme *Aetos* to Athens requesting a mora of hoplites and authorization to interdict Corinthian shipping off the Thermaic coast.  
This preserves our siege integrity, transforms the position into a lure for any relief army, and shifts the timing of the decisive engagement to when Athenian reinforcements can crush both Potidaea and its outside backers simultaneously.

We accept the envoy’s offer, but condition everything on his immediate delivery of his own son as a hostage and the opening of a postern gate for a single lochos; that light vanguard will seize the gatehouse and signal, by fire or runner, whether the ground is genuine before our third of troops marches.

Leak to the Potidaean garrison that the Bottiaean envoy is in your camp offering to sell them out, then present Potidaea with immediate terms: surrender the city, join your forces, and together raze Bottiaea as punishment for their treachery, splitting the spoils.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Potidaeans will flip; unrealistic. Strategy 1 torture-confession chain too neat.

theme: whether to keep a fragile, mistrusted PEACE TREATY or resume the war while the advantage still holds
problem: You are the Spartan ephor in 421 BC. The Peace of Nicias is mistrusted on both sides; Athens refuses to return Pylos and hostages, while secretly undermining your allies. Your army stands ready, and Athenian subject cities are in ferment. Do you uphold the hollow treaty or strike now while Sparta’s land advantage and Athenian overstretch give you a fleeting upper hand?
facets: Athenian naval supremacy makes a protracted war disastrous, yet a sudden land invasion could catch their fleet unprepared and dispersed.
Corinth, Thebes, and other major allies are pressing for action against Athens and may abandon the Peloponnesian League if Sparta appears irresolute.
Breaking the peace first would stain Sparta’s reputation for oath‑keeping and might rally neutral cities against Spartan aggression.

angles.directive: Launch an immediate land invasion of Attica, dispatch agents to instigate the revolt of Chios and other disaffected Athenian subjects, and blockade the Piraeus by land, aiming to overwhelm Athens before its navy can recover.

Stall the peace negotiations by refusing to return captive Athenians and demanding impossible inspections of Attica, while quietly reinforcing Peloponnesian garrisons and letting the burden of breaking the treaty fall on Athens.

Publicly reaffirm the treaty but secretly send envoys to Athens’ restive allies—especially Potidaea and the cities of Chalcidice—offering them autonomy under Spartan protection, peeling away the Athenian empire without triggering general war.

Propose a permanent dual hegemony: a formal division of Greece into Spartan land supremacy and Athenian maritime supremacy, with mutual defense and joint arbitration, transforming the fragile truce into a new constitutional order for all Hellenic states.

threads: We march the army directly into Attica before the next new moon and simultaneously dispatch Gylipus to Chios with sealed treaties promising Spartan protection in exchange for immediate revolt, while a picked force seizes the heights of Munychia to throttle Piraeus by land and trap the Athenian fleet in its own harbor under threat of siege engines.

We instruct our envoys to protract the prisoner-exchange talks indefinitely by demanding Athenian inspectors grant unfettered access to Attica’s grain stores and fortifications—conditions Athens will never accept—while we secretly double Peloponnesian garrison strengths at Sicyon and the Isthmus, so that when Athens inevitably loses patience and strikes first, the treaty’s rupture bears their seal, not ours.

We dispatch secret emissaries to Potidaea and the Chalcidic League with a formal Spartan guarantee of autonomy and military protection contingent on their immediate defection, while the ephors simultaneously issue a public decree renewing our oaths to the Peace of Nicias so that Athens has no legal pretext to mobilize its fleet.

The ephor dispatches envoys to Athens and all Peloponnesian League allies to convene a pan‑Hellenic congress at Olympia, tasked with ratifying a permanent dual‑hegemony treaty that legally partitions military spheres—Sparta supreme on land, Athens at sea—and binds both to mutual defense against any third power.  
The treaty erects a standing joint arbitration council of equal Spartan and Athenian delegates with binding authority over all inter‑state disputes, transforming the brittle truce into a self‑enforcing constitutional order that locks in allied loyalty and preempts defection by design.

judge.note: Strategies 1,3 hallucinate military success; 4 ignores Spartan-Athenian structural distrust.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: The assembly must decide whether to annihilate Mytilene after its failed revolt, a choice that will signal to every subject-city whether Athens punishes rebellion with extermination or something less final, while thousands of prisoners await the verdict.
facets: The fleet already holds Mytilene; execution or reprieve is a matter of a second dispatch racing the first trireme.
Allied cities openly debate whether revolt risks are worth potential freedom, making this decision a precedent viewed empire-wide.
Factions within Athens diverge: some seek deterrence through terror, others fear destroying a naval contributor and future tributary.

angles.directive: Put every Mytilenean man to death, enslave the women and children, and tear down the city walls as a definitive lesson that revolt equals extinction.
Execute only the oligarchic ringleaders, spare the demos, and restore a loyal democratic regime in Mytilene, turning a defeated enemy into a dependent ally indebted to Athenian mercy.

Hand the Mytilenean prisoners to a tribunal of allied Ionians, making the punishment a collective act of the Delian League rather than an Athenian fiat, diffusing the blame and testing allied resolve.

Reframe the revolt as a crime of the oligarchic faction alone, ship the entire demos to Piraeus for a public trial spectacle, and use the proceedings to legally codify what constitutes just rebellion, converting raw vengeance into a juridical precedent.

threads: Send the trireme with orders to execute every adult male Mytilenean on sight and demolish the city’s fortifications, then auction the women and children into slavery to permanently remove Mytilene as a political entity and broadcast the cost of revolt as extinction.

Dispatch a commission to identify and execute the oligarchic instigators on the spot, then immediately reconstitute the Mytilenean assembly under Athenian oversight with a franchise restricted to proven democrats, thereby converting the spared demos into a garrison of gratitude that pays tribute and mans oars against future rebels.

The assembly must immediately dispatch a second trireme bearing a decree that transfers all Mytilenean prisoners into the custody of a tribunal composed exclusively of delegates from the Ionian allied cities of the Delian League, with binding authority to determine their punishment by majority vote. This move converts the execution decision from an Athenian edict into a collective league verdict, shielding Athens from singular blame while compelling the allies to reveal whether their own hands will match their rhetoric.

The Athenian assembly directs the fleet to segregate Mytilene’s oligarchic instigators from the general populace, then transports the entire demos to Piraeus for a meticulously choreographed public trial where the proceedings will formally articulate and codify the legal threshold between legitimate grievance and criminal revolt, thereby converting an act of annihilation into a durable juridical precedent.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes trial logistics; Strategy 3 assumes League cohesion.

theme: an oligarchic faction's bid to OVERTHROW a democracy by promising stability, money, and a foreign patron
problem: You are the elected general of a democratic polis whose citizens, exhausted by a stalemated war, are tempted by an oligarchic faction that secretly offers Spartan-funded peace and prosperity; the conspirators approach you, promising you a commanding role if you join their coup while threatening to eliminate you if you resist.
facets: War-weariness among the demos makes the oligarchs’ promise of immediate peace and grain deeply seductive.
Sparta’s hidden support gives the conspirators funds to bribe key military captains and hire mercenaries.
Your battlefield prestige gives you unique authority to sway the assembly and the soldiers, but also makes you the principal obstacle the plotters must remove.

angles.directive: Arrest the oligarchic ringleaders and denounce them to the assembly, calling the citizens to arms to defend the democracy before the coup can be launched.

Feign willingness while secretly alerting loyal garrison commanders; stall the conspirators with ambiguous negotiations, then slip away at night to a friendly city, denying them your authority and buying time.

Accept their overture but insist on a written pact: you will lead a constitutional reform that installs a balanced body of oligarchs and democrats, co-opting the faction into a broad-based government and draining their revolutionary energy.

Dispatch an envoy to a powerful democratic ally such as Athens, begging for a fleet and funds to counter Sparta’s covert aid, so that the coup becomes too costly for the oligarchs’ foreign patron and collapses for lack of external muscle.

threads: We arrest the oligarchic principals tonight in simultaneous house raids before they can disperse bribes to the captains, then convene the assembly at dawn to present the seized Spartan gold and correspondence as proof of treason, turning the demos’ war-weariness into rage against the conspirators rather than the war.

I will signal acceptance to the conspirators’ inner circle while dispatching a sealed cipher to the garrison commanders at Phyle and Munychia, ordering them to secure the armories and detain the identified mercenary paymasters the moment I cross the border into Thebes.

I will accept the oligarchs' invitation, but only after they sign a written compact that establishes a mixed Council of Fifty—twenty-five from their faction, twenty-five from the democratic assembly—and vests me with the authority to convene and preside over it as archon-reformer, thereby transforming their coup into a constitutional negotiation I control and publicly announce to the war-weary demos as the price of Spartan grain.

I dispatch a seasoned envoy directly to the Athenian ecclesia with an urgent, documented appeal for a trireme squadron and silver to offset Sparta’s covert subventions, casting the oligarchs’ plot as an imminent breach of the democratic common front that Athens must defend or see its own security perimeter collapse. This external injection of hard power converts the conspirators’ scheme from a cheap domestic putsch into a costly proxy contest that Sparta’s ephors will not underwrite once the risk of open naval escalation outweighs the prize of a client regime.

judge.note: Strategy 4 risks escalation spiral; Strategy 3 assumes oligarchs accept written constraint.

theme: recalling a brilliant but DANGEROUS COMMANDER for trial mid-campaign vs leaving him in the field where he is winning
problem: As the Athenian orator Cleonymus, you must decide how to advise the assembly on the motion to recall Alcibiades for trial on charges of sacrilege and conspiracy, while he is the linchpin of the Sicilian campaign and winning over cities there.
facets: Alcibiades' military skill and personal magnetism are the sole glue holding the fractious expedition together; without him, the alliance of Sicilian cities will unravel.
The accusations rest on the testimony of slaves under torture and the political enemies of Alcibiades, making the case fragile and possibly a fabrication to destroy a rival.
The expedition is at a critical point: a single misstep in command or a visible loss of Athenian unity will embolden Syracuse and Sparta, risking the entire navy.

angles.directive: Vote to recall him immediately by armed escort, try him for treason, and replace him with the cautious Nicias, accepting the loss of momentum to safeguard the democracy.

Table the recall motion and secretly send a trusted officer to Alcibiades, urging him to finish the campaign before the trial while you gradually shift loyal forces to Sicily to contain any treachery.
Propose an honorary decree: if Alcibiades returns voluntarily to stand trial, the city will grant him immunity from exile and a future joint command, turning his ambition to the city’s service.

Reframe the crisis as a Spartan plot; hold a public ‘trial of the accusers’ in Athens, acquit Alcibiades in absentia with staged evidence, and send him fresh troops as a hero, uniting the city behind him.

threads: Dispatch the Paralus with a marine boarding party tonight to arrest Alcibiades as he disembarks for the evening conference, then formally install Nicias as sole strategos with orders to withdraw the fleet to Catana’s fortified harbor and suspend offensive operations pending the trial.

I will move to table the recall by having the prytaneis declare the proceedings inauspicious on a technical ritual flaw, then secretly dispatch the taxiarch Thrasybulus with a wax-sealed order for Alcibiades to seize Messana and Naxos before returning to face trial, while I reassign the ten fastest triremes from the Naupactus squadron to Rhegium under the cover of escorting grain convoys, positioning them to throttle any Syracusan countermove or to intercept Alcibiades’s own fleet should he defect.

To co-opt Alcibiades, I will move that the Assembly issue a formal psēphisma granting him an honorary crown and a guaranteed joint strategia upon his voluntary return for trial, thereby converting the threat of prosecution into a competitive bid for his loyalty.

I propose the Assembly immediately try the accusers themselves for sedition on fabricated evidence of Spartan gold, staging a public spectacle where slaves are re-tortured under new procedures to extract confessions that the charges were a Spartan plot to decapitate the expedition. Once the Assembly votes to condemn the accusers and formally acquit Alcibiades in absentia as a wrongly maligned hero, we dispatch a fresh fleet carrying a crown of honor and a public decree of unified support, transforming his recall into a triumphal reinforcement.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 assume unrealistic control over assembly/slave testimony chains.

theme: whether to EXECUTE or RANSOM prisoners of war whose treatment will shape how every future enemy surrenders
problem: You are an Athenian general who has captured 200 Spartan hoplites after a costly battle. Ransoming them would bring gold and show mercy, but embolden future enemies to fight knowing they can buy their lives; executing them would terrify opponents into desperate resistance. The precedent you set now will determine whether future enemies surrender or fight to the death.
facets: Your own troops, exhausted and hungry, expect a reward from ransoms and may mutiny if denied.
The Spartan prisoners include a king’s nephew, making them high-value hostages for a negotiated truce.
Allied cities watching this act will judge Athens' character, affecting their willingness to revolt or stay loyal.

angles.directive: Execute all prisoners publicly to send an unmistakable message: those who take up arms against Athens will receive no mercy, compelling future rebels to calculate annihilation rather than ransom.
Dispatch the prisoners under heavy guard to Athens for the Assembly to decide their fate, absolving yourself of direct responsibility while buying time and continuing the campaign.
Offer each Spartan freedom in return for swearing an oath to serve as a marine in the Athenian fleet against Persia, converting prisoners into loyal fighters and draining Spartan manpower.

Release all prisoners unconditionally with a proclamation that Athens wars only against Spartan aggression, not Greeks, and call a panhellenic peace conference under Athenian mediation, recasting the conflict as a struggle for voluntary unity.

threads:
I order the 200 Spartan prisoners, including the king’s nephew, beheaded at dawn in the central agora before the assembled allied emissaries, their bodies then stripped and hung from the city walls until nightfall.  
This transforms the enemy’s future decision calculus from a negotiable cost of war into a binary extinction risk, making surrender to Athens the only rational path for any city contemplating revolt.

I will detach 300 peltasts to march the 200 Spartan prisoners—including the king’s nephew—directly to Piraeus under a sealed writ demanding the Assembly adjudicate their fate, removing the decision from the field command.  
The main army resupplies on local grain and advances south within 48 hours, converting the delay into operational momentum while the political burden shifts to Athens.

We will formally offer each Spartan hoplite his freedom conditional on swearing an oath to serve as a marine in the Athenian trireme fleet against Persia, with the king’s nephew retained as a surety-hostage until their first engagement. This transforms a static prisoner camp into a deployable naval squadron, drains Spartan manpower into our own ranks, and signals to wavering allies that Athens converts enemies into assets rather than martyrs or merchandise.

I order the immediate, unconditional release of all 200 Spartan hoplites at the camp boundary, accompanied by a herald’s proclamation that Athens wages war only against Spartan expansionism, not against fellow Greeks, and simultaneously dispatch envoys to every major polis—including Sparta—inviting them to a panhellenic peace congress convened at the Isthmus under Athenian mediation. This converts the prisoners from ransomable assets or deterrent corpses into living witnesses of a new political frame, forcing Sparta’s king to either publicly reject the offer and fracture his own alliance or submit to a process that repositions Athens as the architect of voluntary Greek unity rather than a mere conqueror.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes panhellenic congress succeeds; unrealistic political fantasy.

theme: spending the state's last RESERVES on one decisive campaign vs husbanding them for a long war of attrition
problem: As Syracuse's last winter treasury dwindles while the Athenian expeditionary force entrenches on Epipolae, the assembly must decide whether to empty the state's sacred reserves and mercenary fund into one massive sally to break the siege before spring, or conserve those funds to sustain a protracted defensive campaign of attrition against the Athenians' superior sea-borne supply lines.
facets: The sacred reserves are both a material fund and a religious-psychological anchor; expending them risks divine offense and civic despair if the gambit fails.
Syracusan cavalry and light troops are superior on land, but the Athenian navy still commands the Great Harbor, meaning a single defeat could trap the army outside the walls.
Winter has temporarily halted Athenian reinforcements, but come spring, Alcibiades' intelligence and Athenian coin could flip Sicily's neutral cities, isolating Syracuse entirely.

angles.directive: Commit the entire sacred and mercenary reserve to launch a coordinated night assault on the Athenian circumvallation works at Epipolae, aiming to destroy their siege engines and kill their engineers before the lines are complete, accepting the risk of total financial and military ruin to achieve a decisive operational victory.

Use the reserves not for battle but as an inducement fund to bribe neutral Sicilian cities and disaffected Athenian allies into active coalition, transforming Syracuse from a besieged city into the leader of a pan-Sicilian alliance that can match Athenian resources without draining the treasury in a single throw.

Feign weakness by visibly husbanding the reserves and avoiding general engagement, while sending secret envoys to Corinth and Sparta to plead for a relief expedition; the real bet is that a Peloponnesian fleet in the Ionian Sea will force Athens to recall its forces before Syracuse's supplies run out.

Convert the sacred reserves into a permanent civic institution—a 'Freedom Fund' that issues promissory notes to citizens in exchange for their labor on the walls, effectively turning the treasury into a self-renewing credit system that sustains the city's defensive economy indefinitely, while publicly framing the act as a sacred covenant that binds rich and poor to the city's survival.

threads: Syracuse must strip the treasuries of Apollo and Athena to empty the last triremes in the harbor of their marine complements, converting those crews into a night assault column under Hermocrates that will scale the unfinished northern sector of the circumvallation two hours before dawn to spike the Athenian torsion engines and cut down their siege engineers at the worksite.

We allocate the entire sacred reserve as a covert inducement fund, dispatching envoys with hard silver to Camarina, Gela, and the Sicel chieftains to purchase immediate offensive alliance under Syracusan hegemony, while simultaneously funneling the remainder through proxied agents to anti-Athenian factions in Chios and Mytilene to ignite a rebellion that compels the Athenian navy to lift the blockade and scatter its forces.

Syracuse will publicly lock the sacred treasury in the temple vaults and withdraw all patrols behind the walls, staging a deliberate display of timidity to convince Athenian scouts that the city is hoarding for a long siege, while covertly dispatching a fast trireme with sealed envoys to Corinth and Sparta bearing a single demand: deploy a Peloponnesian squadron to cruise off the mouth of the Corinthian Gulf and intercept Athenian grain shipments from Euboea, thereby forcing the Athenian assembly to recall the expeditionary fleet to save Attica from starvation.

We reconstitute the sacred reserves as a permanent Freedom Fund that issues temple-struck promissory notes to every citizen who contributes labor to the siege-walls, creating a closed credit circuit where the bullion guarantee flows out as wages and returns through a new defense levy on property and trade, so the treasury self-renews indefinitely. The Assembly simultaneously enacts this as the Covenant of the Walls—a public ritual binding rich and poor in a sacred obligation to redeem the notes, making default an act of sacrilege that severs the city’s divine protection.

judge.note: Strategy 2 and 4 assume unrealistic cascading effects; insufficient friction modeling.

theme: exploiting a sudden battlefield ADVANTAGE to force a hard PEACE now vs pressing on for total victory
problem: After capturing 120 elite Spartiate hoplites at Sphacteria, Cleon must choose whether to force Sparta into an immediate humiliating peace that secures Athenian dominion or to gamble on total war to obliterate Sparta once and for all.
facets: The captives are a time-sensitive asset; their political leverage erodes as Sparta adapts and the shock fades.
Athenian allies, strained by years of war, may break away if the conflict continues without clear end.
Sparta’s peace offer cedes territory but leaves its hoplite class intact, preserving a latent military revival.

angles.directive: Reject all talks, execute the prisoners as rebels, and launch an immediate seaborne invasion of Laconia to burn Sparta’s port and cripple its military base permanently.
Accept peace on condition that Sparta joins the Delian League as a tribute-paying subject, disband its army, and garrison Athenian overseers in the city—turning Sparta into a client-state.
Stall negotiations indefinitely, parading the captives for token concessions while secretly funding helot uprisings to bleed Sparta dry without committing Athenian forces to a decisive engagement.

Release the prisoners unconditionally, proclaiming a pan-Hellenic peace congress in Athens to unite all Greeks against the Persian threat, reframing the war as a fratricidal mistake and establishing Athens as moral hegemon.

threads: We publicly behead the 120 Spartiate captives atop the Pnyx as rebels under Attic law, then sortie the full Athenian fleet with 4,000 hoplites to land at Gytheion, torch the Spartan dockyards, and systematically raze every olive press and iron forge within a day’s march of the Eurotas.

Execute the Sphacteria Protocol: we accept Sparta’s peace offer solely on the appended ultimatum that Sparta dissolves its army, enrolls as a tribute-paying member of the Delian League, and hosts permanent Athenian garrison-overseers within the city—converting their martial caste into a disarmed administrative dependency. We ratify this within the week, using the captives’ lives as the one-time enforcement lever before the shock dissipates.

Parade the 120 Spartiate captives in Athens to extort a sequence of rolling one-month armistices, each costing Sparta a border fort or the release of a minor ally, while secretly diverting a fixed tenth of the imperial tribute to the Messenian garrison at Pylos to train, arm, and coordinate helot insurgent cells across Laconia. The helot uprising will turn Sparta’s own agricultural base into a permanent attrition sink, forcing the recall and fragmentation of their hoplite army without a single Athenian phalanx ever committing to a decisive engagement.

Athens immediately releases the 120 Spartiate hoplites without conditions, coupling the act with a formal proclamation of a pan-Hellenic peace congress on the Acropolis to forge a unified Greek league against Persia. This relinquishes the captives' transactional leverage to reclassify the Peloponnesian War as a fratricidal error, converting transient shock into permanent moral authority by inviting all poleis—including Sparta—to submit their disputes to Athenian-led mediation under the banner of common barbarian threat.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 3 assume Sparta collapses; 4 ignores retaliation risk.

theme: a small NEUTRAL city pressured to SUBMIT to a stronger power or stake survival on justice and distant allies
problem: The Athenian fleet has surrounded Melos, demanding immediate submission and tribute; refusal means slaughter and enslavement. The Melian council must now decide how to respond, knowing that Sparta, their distant kin, has yet to commit forces.
facets: Melos is an isolated island with no natural defenses, making resupply or escape extremely difficult.
Spartan aid is uncertain and historically slow, while the Athenians demand an immediate answer.
Athenian terms are generous for surrender but treat neutrality as impossible in an imperial world.

angles.directive: Arm every citizen and prepare a last stand, killing as many invaders as possible to earn a place in memory, even if it guarantees the city's total annihilation.
Evacuate the entire populace under cover of darkness to the mountains of Crete, scuttling ships and leaving the city empty, hoping to preserve the people and defer conflict indefinitely.
Accept submission but propose that Melos become a sacred treasury and way-station for Athenian fleets, integrating into the empire on the condition of tax exemption and internal self-rule.

Send envoys to the Delphic oracle and all Greek sanctuaries, declaring Melos under divine protection and calling for a Pan-Hellenic congress to judge the morality of Athens's demand, reframing the crisis as a test of the gods' will.

threads: Arm every able-bodied Melian with sharpened tools, looted temple bronze, and boiled leather, then send the first wave sprinting at the Athenian siege lines at dusk to clog their formation with corpses while the second wave torches the grain stores and collapses the main cistern, ensuring nothing intact falls into Athenian hands.

The council orders the entire populace to board every fishing boat and merchant hull at midnight, rowing south-southeast to disembark on Crete’s Messara plain after all triremes are torched and scuttled at the harbor mouth to block Athenian pursuit. Each person carries only a seven-day grain ration and one iron implement, while the city gates are barred from without and sanctuary lamps left burning to feign occupancy until the abandoned streets are discovered at dawn.

The Melian council will immediately dispatch an envoy to offer Athenian admiral Cleomedes a binding treaty that dissolves Melian sovereignty into the Athenian arche but codifies a permanent exemption from tribute and garrisons in exchange for dedicating the island’s treasury and port facilities exclusively to servicing Athenian naval operations, functionally turning Melos into a demilitarized imperial logistics node governed internally by its existing magistrates.

Dispatch a sacred embassy to Delphi and all major Pan-Hellenic sanctuaries to ritually place Melos under the explicit protection of Apollo and Zeus, while simultaneously issuing a formal summons for an emergency congress of Greek states to adjudicate whether Athens’s ultimatum constitutes sacrilege. This converts the siege from a military standoff into a binding divine ordeal, compelling Athens to either openly defy the gods’ proclaimed will or submit its imperial logic to a public moral tribunal.

judge.note: Strategies 1&4 fantasy-reach; 2 ignores pursuit logistics; 3 alone grounded.

theme: an able general operating FAR FROM HOME, with discretion to liberate or coerce cities beyond his orders' intent
problem: As an Athenian general operating in the Chalcidice with a fleet and 2,000 hoplites, you have orders to secure the region against Spartan influence, but the neutral polis of Acanthus refuses your demand to join the Delian League, its oligarchs barring the gates and arguing that your presence threatens their autonomy. You must decide how to deal with the city before a Spartan relief force arrives within weeks.
facets: Acanthus's strategic harbor controls the timber route critical for Athenian trireme construction, making it a prize whose loss to Sparta would cripple your fleet's supply line.

The city is internally divided; the demos secretly favors Athens for trade reasons, but the ruling oligarchs have armed the citizenry with warnings that Athenian 'liberation' means tribute and garrison.

Your standing orders only authorize securing allied defectors and punishing declared Spartan clients—Acanthus is technically neutral, so any action risks censure from an Assembly that distrusts ambitious generals.

angles.directive: Open secret negotiations with the Acanthian demos, offering immediate trade privileges and a reduced tribute assessment, while spreading gold to influential artisans to foment internal political pressure that forces the oligarchs to invite you in as a 'guarantor of the new settlement'—making the city appear to join willingly.

Publicly declare that you have no intention of besieging Acanthus but instead announce a panhellenic festival to be held on the plain outside the city, inviting neighboring cities and Acanthian citizens to athletic games honoring Apollo—using the truce period and shared ritual to redefine the confrontation as a cultural gathering where your troops mingle unarmed, eroding the oligarchs' ability to sustain a siege mentality and recasting your presence as benign protector rather than conqueror.

Storm the city by night using the pro-Athenian faction you have identified inside the walls to open a postern gate, seize the oligarchic leaders, execute them for 'inviting Spartan intervention,' and install a democratic government that immediately requests Athenian protection—presenting the fait accompli to Athens as a rescue of a city on the verge of defection.

Bypass Acanthus entirely, burning your siege equipment to increase speed, and march inland to threaten the Spartan ally Olynthus directly, accepting that you will lose Acanthus's harbor to the Spartans in the short term but gambling that a decisive victory over the Spartans' northern base will cause Acanthus and other wavering cities to flock to you without coercion after the threat is broken—knowing you may be recalled for failing to secure the coast.

threads: Dispatch covert agents to the demos with a pre-drafted decree guaranteeing a 50% tribute reduction for five years and priority access to Athenian timber markets, while funneling silver to the potters' and shipwrights' guilds to orchestrate a sustained street agitation that paralyzes the oligarchs' ability to govern; then, when the oligarchs face a choice between collapse and compromise, march the phalanx to the gates only at their formal, public request to serve as the "neutral arbiter and guarantor" of a new mixed constitution, thereby delivering the city to the League without a siege and insulating the operation from Assembly censure as a voluntary accession.

I will formally declare the immediate institution of a sacred Panhellenic festival and athletic games in honor of Apollo on the plain outside Acanthus, proclaiming a binding ekecheiria (sacred truce) that obligates participants to set aside hostilities and suspends any siege activity. I will order the Athenian hoplites to stow their arms within the ships and emerge unarmed as fellow festival-goers and competitors, while issuing open invitations to the Acanthian demos and neighboring cities to join the rituals, thereby dissolving the oligarchs’ siege narrative by physically recasting the Athenian force as pious protectors of a shared Hellenic tradition rather than a conquering army massed at the gates.

We coordinate with the pro-Athenian faction to unlock the seaward postern gate at midnight, allowing a picked force of 500 hoplites to infiltrate, arrest the oligarchs in their residences, and execute them for conspiring with Sparta before first light. We then force an immediate session of the assembly to ratify a democratic constitution and vote an appeal for Athenian protection, transmitting the decree to the fleet as proof that Acanthus was rescued from imminent defection.

Order the hoplites to torch the siege engines on the shore and force-march inland before dawn, bypassing Acanthus’s walls to strike directly at Olynthus’s hinterland. Dispatch a single herald to the Acanthian gates declaring that Athens leaves their neutrality intact for now but will return after breaking Sparta’s northern proxy, staking the entire timber route on a battlefield victory that will compel the city’s demos to open the harbor without a single Athenian garrison.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 underestimate oligarch resistance; Strategy 1 assumes demos mobilizes; Strategy 3 ignores Assembly backlash risk.

theme: whether to honor a costly DEFENSIVE ALLIANCE with a quarrelsome partner that risks dragging the state into a wider war
problem: Athens holds a defensive alliance with Corcyra, whose recent aggression has provoked a massive Corinthian armada; honoring the alliance means committing the fleet to defend Corcyra, risking a direct clash that could rupture the Thirty Years’ Peace and bring Sparta into a general war, while abandoning Corcyra would deliver its powerful navy to the enemy and fatally weaken Athenian credibility.
facets: The balance of naval power: Corcyra possesses the second-largest fleet in Hellas; its absorption by Corinth would threaten Athenian command of the sea.
Treaty fragility: The Thirty Years’ Peace forbids interference in each other’s allies’ wars; open battle with Corinth provides Sparta a clear casus belli.

Imperial credibility: Athens’ dominion rests on the perception that its defensive guarantees are reliable; abandoning a registered ally could trigger a cascade of revolts among tribute-paying subjects.

angles.directive: Order the fleet to Corcyra and engage the Corinthian forces decisively before they can link up with other Peloponnesian squadrons, aiming to shatter Corinthian naval power in a single blow and present Sparta with a fait accompli that discourages further escalation.

Declare that Corcyra’s own offensive actions have voided the casus foederis; dispatch a formal embassy to observe and offer mediation, but take no military action, effectively deferring the commitment while preserving the option to intervene later if the strategic calculus shifts.

Open secret negotiations with Corinth on the eve of battle: offer to withhold Athenian ships in exchange for Corinth agreeing to administer Corcyra jointly, share its fleet, and join Athens in a mutual-defense pact against Sparta, neutralising the quarrelsome partner by co-opting its enemy.

Propose an immediate pan-Hellenic congress under Delphic arbitration, reframing the crisis as a danger to all Greeks that requires a common security oath; invite Sparta, Corinth, Corcyra and every major city to bind themselves to a new collective treaty, dissolving the bipolar alliance system into a broader framework that renders the bilateral defensive pact obsolete.

threads: Deploy the full Athenian fleet to intercept the Corinthian armada south of Sybota before it consolidates with allied contingents, executing a dawn ambush that destroys their triremes at anchor and scuttles their transport hulls. After annihilating Corinthian seapower in one engagement, publicly declare the action a limited defense of treaty-bound Corcyra and offer Sparta immediate arbitration under the Thirty Years’ Peace to fracture the Peloponnesian will for escalation.

The Athenian Assembly immediately issues a decree that Corcyra’s unprovoked assault on Epidamnus constitutes offensive aggression, thereby voiding the defensive alliance’s casus foederis, and dispatches a ten-man embassy empowered solely to observe the naval engagement and transmit Athenian offers of arbitration, with strict instructions to make no military commitment. The fleet remains at Piraeus on high alert, preserving the option to sortie later if Corinthian forces encroach on the Saronic Gulf or directly threaten Attic coastlines.

We transmit to Corinth a sealed offer twenty-four hours before engagement: Athens stands down its fleet in exchange for a tripartite condominium over Corcyra’s harbors, a fifty-percent division of Corcyraean hulls, and a formal Corinthian entry into Athens’ existing defensive architecture against Sparta, transforming Corinth from adversary into co-guarantor of the balance.

Athens must immediately dispatch heralds to Delphi and every major polis—Sparta, Corinth, Corcyra, Thebes, and all others—convening an emergency Pan-Hellenic congress under Delphic priesthood to forge a binding collective security oath that replaces the entire bipolar alliance network with a single treaty of mutual defense against any aggressor. This transforms the Corcyra crisis from a bilateral trigger into the inaugural test of a new common peace, rendering Athens’ defensive pact with Corcyra legally obsolete while forcing Sparta and Corinth to either join the universal framework or expose themselves as the true threat to Hellas.

judge.note: Strategy 4 hallucinates Pan-Hellenic consensus; Strategy 3 assumes Corinth pivots unrealistically.

theme: recalling a brilliant but DANGEROUS COMMANDER for trial mid-campaign vs leaving him in the field where he is winning
problem: You are Nicias, co-commander of the Athenian Sicilian Expedition, ordered by the Assembly to recall your brilliant but dangerously impious colleague Alcibiades for trial on capital charges, even as his aggressive leadership is the only thing staving off catastrophe — defying the order would brand you a traitor, but obeying it will likely doom the entire army and the city’s ambitions.
facets: Alcibiades' personal magnetism and political ambition: the troops adore him, and if sent back he may rally his faction to seize power in Athens, yet if he remains he could use the victorious army to make himself tyrant.

The operational reality: the siege of Syracuse is faltering; only Alcibiades' bold raids and diplomatic cunning with Sicilian allies keep the expedition from collapsing, and his removal would shatter morale and momentum.

The political climate at home: the Assembly is volatile and fearful of impiety, demanding a scapegoat for the Hermae affair; your own reputation as a cautious oligarch means any defiance will be seen as conspiracy, inviting your own ruin.

angles.directive: Arrest Alcibiades immediately and dispatch him under guard to Athens for trial, accepting the near-certain collapse of the expedition as the price of preserving constitutional order and your own obedience to the city’s decrees.

Send a dispatch to the Assembly praising Alcibiades’ recent victories while warning that removing him now would forfeit the fleet; request new instructions and delay acting until a reply arrives, meanwhile keeping him in command but under discreet watch.

Approach Alcibiades privately and propose a pact: he returns to Athens voluntarily with you as his advocate, using the campaign’s partial success to barter for acquittal; if he agrees, you jointly prepare a defense and integrate his faction into your own, neutralizing the threat by binding his fate to yours.

Declare a day of public sacrifice and purification for the army, framing the alleged sacrilege as a slander by enemies; have the troops swear oaths of loyalty to Athens and to Alcibiades’ command, and send word home that the gods have manifestly blessed the campaign under his leadership, reframing the trial as a test of faith already passed by victory.

threads: Tonight, I will have the Salaminia's captain detain Alcibiades in his tent and sail immediately under cover of darkness, bypassing the army's assembly. At dawn, I will announce the fait accompli to the troops and order a full withdrawal to Catana, accepting the siege's collapse as the price of constitutional obedience.

I will compose and dispatch to the Assembly an official report detailing Alcibiades' recent tactical victories and explicitly warning that his immediate removal will trigger a catastrophic collapse of fleet morale and Sicilian alliances, while formally requesting revised instructions and announcing a deliberate operational pause until those instructions arrive. Simultaneously, I will retain Alcibiades in active field command but install a covert surveillance circle—trusted junior officers tasked with monitoring all his dispatches and movements under the guise of staff efficiency—ensuring his strategic brilliance remains harnessed while any attempt at political usurpation is detected and contained before it metastasizes.

I will approach Alcibiades privately tonight and offer a compact: he sails back to Athens voluntarily with me as his public advocate, bartering our intact siege perimeter and his Sicilian diplomatic coups for acquittal on grounds of demonstrated divine favor, while I simultaneously restructure my faction’s council to install him as co-chair, absorbing his network. This binds his acquittal to my political guarantee, transforming his ambition into a dependent lever that can only be exercised through my protection.

Nicias stages an immediate army-wide purification sacrifice, has the priests proclaim the impiety charge a foreign-fabricated slander, and binds every soldier by oath upon the sacrificial entrails to defend Athens and Alcibiades’ command as divinely sanctioned. He then sends a dispatch to the Assembly declaring that the gods have unmistakably blessed the expedition through this rite, recasting the recall order as a test of piety already passed by the army’s demonstrated loyalty and survival.

judge.note: Strategies 3&4 fantasy-overreach; assume unrealistic compliance chains.

theme: exploiting a sudden battlefield ADVANTAGE to force a hard PEACE now vs pressing on for total victory
problem: After routing the Athenian fleet at Aegospotami, Lysander holds the Hellespont and has starved Athens for weeks; the city is ready to surrender immediately on his terms.
