Accepting their surrender now guarantees Spartan hegemony but leaves the Long Walls standing and the Athenian imperial system only humbled, not destroyed — pressing on to total victory means besieging and razing Athens itself, risking re-kindled resistance, Corinthian/Theban demands for Athens’ annihilation, and Persian second thoughts.
facets: Allies (Corinth, Thebes) demand the obliteration of Athens, while Spartan tradition favors a moderate settlement among Greeks.
A protracted siege risks giving the Athenian fleet remnants time to regroup or Persia to withdraw financial support, but an immediate peace leaves a potential rival intact.
Lysander’s personal authority, his network of dekarchies, and his relationship with Cyrus of Persia all hang on whether he is seen to deliver final, irreversible victory or merely a negotiated pause.

angles.directive: Reject the Athenian surrender, storm the city, raze the Long Walls to music of flutes, and execute the political leadership — eliminating Athens as a rival power forever, whatever the cost in time, allied pressure, and Persian patience.

Grant Athens immediate peace with honor — spare the walls, leave a garrison on the Acropolis, but install an obedient oligarchy of Ten (later Thirty) personally loyal to you, turning Athens into a client enforcer of Spartan hegemony under your own network, not an independent polis.

Accept the surrender in principle but defer the final terms to the ephors and the Spartan assembly — frame your battlefield advantage as a mandate for a pan-Hellenic congress, shifting the risk of a destructive sack or an over-generous peace away from your personal command and onto the institutions of Sparta and her allies.

Reframe total victory not as annihilation but as constitutional dismemberment: dictate a peace that physically separates Athens into independent village-cantons (dioikismos), dissolves the Delian League permanently into a Spartan-led maritime league, and transplants the Athenian navy’s remaining hulls to Sparta — not a punitive sack but a permanent redesign of the Greek state-system that removes the very category of 'Athenian empire'.

threads: Lysander immediately rejects the Athenian envoy’s offer, deploys the full Spartan fleet to seal Piraeus by sea while dispatching a picked force of helots and allied hoplites to breach the Long Walls from the landward side at the Dipylon Gate under cover of a moonless night assault. Once inside, his men systematically dismantle the fortifications to the sound of flutes, execute every identified strategos, trierarch, and outspoken demagogue of the Athenian assembly, and impale their bodies along the Ceramicus as a permanent marker that the polis of Athens is now a Spartan satrapy in all but name.

Accept Athens’ immediate surrender now, bypassing allied extermination demands, and lock in Spartan hegemony by sparing the Long Walls while planting a permanent garrison on the Acropolis and installing a handpicked decarchy—later the Thirty—drawn from exiles and oligarchs personally bound to you through patronage, not polis loyalty. This converts Athens from a rival naval power into a client enforcer whose military infrastructure remains intact only to serve your network’s dekarchies across the Aegean, securing Persian confidence by delivering a stable, subordinate terminus for Cyrus’s supply lines without the risk of a prolonged siege rekindling resistance.

Lysander accepts Athens’ surrender in principle by issuing an immediate armistice but formally defers all permanent terms to a pan-Hellenic congress convened by the ephors and the Spartan assembly, citing the need for collective allied sanction. He thereby transforms his battlefield advantage into a procedural mandate, insulating his personal authority and dekarchic network from the consequences of either annihilating Athens or leaving it armed, while shifting the risk of Corinthian/Theban demands and Persian second thoughts onto the institutional shoulders of Sparta and its congress.

Lysander will dictate a peace that decrees the *dioikismos* of Athens into ten autonomous village-cantons, dissolves the Delian League by treaty and reconstitutes it as a permanent Spartan-led maritime league with all allied fleets conscripted under Spartan navarchy, and physically transfers every remaining Athenian trireme hull to Gytheion to be recommissioned under Lacedaemonian commanders. This constitutional dismemberment eliminates the category “Athenian empire” entirely by erasing its metropolitan center, its institutional fleet, and its tributary network in a single legal stroke, leaving no entity to revive and making Corinthian/Theban demands for annihilation irrelevant because Athens as a unitary imperial polis will have ceased to exist.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 fantasy-overreach; 3 evades rather than commits.

theme: launching a distant, glittering FOREIGN EXPEDITION that overextends the state vs consolidating strength at home
problem: As Nicias, appointed commander of the Sicilian Expedition, you must decide how to wield the massive Athenian fleet anchored at Corcyra, bound for Syracuse. The assembly expects conquest, but you judge the whole enterprise an overextending folly that risks the empire.
facets: The Athenian assembly, drunk on ambition, has voted overwhelming resources and will brook no retreat.
The Sicilian cities are divided but Syracuse is a formidable, wealthy rival that could unite the island against invaders.
Sparta, though bound by a fragile peace, watches for any Athenian weakness to strike.

angles.directive: Lead the expedition with maximum aggression, attacking Syracuse directly to shatter its power before it can mobilize.
Evade command by feigning illness or insisting on impossibly heavy reinforcements to delay departure until the assembly cancels the venture.
Co-opt neutral Sicilian cities with promises of autonomy and shared plunder, weaving a coalition to isolate Syracuse diplomatically.
Transform the expedition’s purpose: sail, demonstrate Athenian might along the coasts, and offer Syracuse a generous peace, turning a war of conquest into a short, face-saving show.

threads: We land the entire expeditionary force at dawn directly on Ortygia's shore inside the Great Harbour, breaching the walls with rams and massed hoplites before Syracuse can form a phalanx. Simultaneously, a picked marine detachment seizes the Arethusa spring and torches the granaries, collapsing the city's resistance within the week.

I dispatch a formal letter to the Athenian Assembly, demanding an immediate reinforcement of 100 additional triremes and 5,000 hoplites, explicitly asserting that without this impossible levy the expedition faces certain annihilation. Simultaneously, I stage a public medical crisis—claiming a severe renal inflammation that renders me bedridden—so that the fleet at Corcyra stagnates, the season closes, and the ecclesia is forced to either cancel the venture or impeach its own chosen commander.

I will dispatch envoys to the neutral Chalcidian poleis of Naxos, Catana, and Leontini with a ratified Athenian decree guaranteeing their perpetual autonomy, a binding defensive alliance, and a fixed one-third share of all Syracusan spoils in return for basing rights and their levies to encircle the city. This isolates Syracuse inside a hostile diplomatic ring, forcing it to fight without allies or negotiate from a position of systemic weakness before the fleet ever commits to siege.

Nicias will sail the Athenian fleet in a full-dress procession along the Sicilian coast, anchoring at prominent cities to disembark envoys rather than hoplites, and will dispatch a herald to Syracuse carrying a formal offer of alliance on terms that demand only symbolic tribute and a mutual non-aggression pact, explicitly renouncing any claim to Sicilian territory. This converts the armada from an invasion force into a coercive diplomatic spectacle, using the assembly’s sunk cost in ships and men to extract a face-saving accommodation that preempts both a Syracusan-led coalition and a Spartan intervention.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Syracuse accepts humiliation; Strategy 2 ignores assembly backlash.

theme: spending the state's last RESERVES on one decisive campaign vs husbanding them for a long war of attrition
problem: As Syracuse's general, you have one untouched war-chest and a newly arrived Athenian expeditionary force besieging your city; you must decide whether to commit your entire remaining reserves to a single pitched naval battle in the Great Harbour this week, or conserve those funds to outlast the Athenians in a protracted siege war of attrition.
facets: Athenian supply lines are stretched across the sea and winter is approaching, making their position logistically fragile over time.
Your mercenary rowers and allied squadrons are restive and will only stay loyal if paid regularly, which prolonged war demands.
A victory now would shatter Athenian morale and trigger political consequences in Athens, while a defeat would leave Syracuse utterly defenseless.

angles.directive: Spend everything on a sudden, all-out naval assault within the week, blocking the harbour mouth with chained triremes to prevent Athenian escape and aiming to annihilate their fleet in a single decisive engagement.

Avoid battle entirely; dismantle the Athenian camp's forward palisades at night, withdraw your fleet into the inner harbour beyond their reach, and use the war-chest to bribe deserters and delay until the enemy exhausts itself.

Use a portion of the reserve to open secret negotiations with the Athenian fleet's allied and mercenary contingents, offering immediate cash payment to defect or at least stand aside, thereby hollowing out the enemy from within before any battle.

Publicly announce you will spend the reserve not on war but on a spectacular sacrifice to Apollo and a grand festival of deliverance, framing the siege as a divine test that the city will endure through spiritual unity, forcing the Athenians to assault prepared fortifications or lose face and time.

threads: Commit the entire war-chest tonight to settle all rower arrears and issue a one-time victory bonus, then at dawn launch every battle-ready trireme in a crescent formation that seals the harbour mouth with six triremes chained bow-to-stern as a floating barrier, driving the Athenian fleet against the shallows to be rammed and burned to the waterline.

Send sappers tonight to dismantle the Athenian forward palisades under cover of darkness, collapsing their outer defensive line while the fleet slips silently into the inner harbour beyond ramming reach. Divert the untouched war-chest into a standing bounty for deserters and targeted bribes to allied captains, letting starvation and winter fracture their expedition before we spend a single hull.

I will dispatch a trusted agent with one-seventh of the war-chest to the Athenian fleet’s Megarian and Corinthian marine contingents via the neutral Akragantine trader who already supplies their cook-fires, offering each trierarch an immediate cash bounty in weighed silver for every ship that drops anchor outside the harbour on the morning of our choosing. Simultaneously, I will have my Syracusan seamen circulate a forged despatch from the Athenian strategos, “intercepted” and copied, implying that allied crews will be paid only after the city falls, so that the offer lands on a fault line already cracking under pay uncertainty.

I order the entire untouched war-chest melted into a golden hecatomb for Apollo and a week-long public Festival of Deliverance proclaimed citywide, reframing the siege as a divine ordeal that Syracuse will survive through sacred unity, not silver. This forces the Athenian fleet to either hurl itself against our fortified seawalls before winter storms sever their supply line or retreat in disgrace, while our citizen hoplites and sailors take oaths to hold the ramparts unpaid, leaving the mercenaries to be placated by spectacle and the promise of divine—not monetary—reward.

judge.note: Strategy 3 bribes assume predictable defection; Strategy 4 assumes mercenaries accept spectacle over pay.

theme: an able general operating FAR FROM HOME, with discretion to liberate or coerce cities beyond his orders' intent
problem: An Athenian general operating in Ionia, far from home, finds a strategically vital neutral city whose oligarchic rulers secretly favor Sparta. He must decide whether to assault its walls, bypass it for other targets, co-opt the rulers with a negotiated settlement, or trigger a democratic uprising—each path risking the loss of his fleet, the alienation of allies, or the wrath of the assembly back home.
facets: The city's formidable walls and the general's limited siege train, making a direct assault a gamble with high casualties and no certainty of success.
The internal political divide: an entrenched oligarchic faction that controls the city but faces a restive democratic population, neither side fully reliable.
The general's ambiguous orders from Athens, which grant him discretion to secure the region but leave him exposed to blame if he fails or overreaches.

angles.directive: Launch a surprise assault before the oligarchs can summon Spartan aid, accepting high casualties to remove the threat decisively and present Athens with a fait accompli.
Withdraw and target softer coastal towns, leaving the city isolated but untouched, hoping to pressure it later without a fight while preserving the fleet for more winnable battles.

Offer the oligarchs a secret pact guaranteeing their power and trade privileges in exchange for strict neutrality, thus securing the city without a battle and avoiding a contentious democratic experiment.
Secretly arm the democratic faction to overthrow the rulers, turning the city into a staunch Athenian ally through regime change, even if it risks a prolonged civil war and reprisals.

threads: Launch a nocturnal escalade at the steepest—and thus least guarded—section of the curtain wall with picked men in darkness, bypassing the gates entirely to seize the inner citadel and decapitate the oligarchic council in their chambers before dawn.

The general will bypass the oligarchic stronghold entirely, sailing the fleet south to systematically reduce the unfortified coastal settlements of Teos and Lebedos, seizing their grain stores and imposing democratic garrisons to deny the city its supply lines and regional influence without a single assault on its walls.

Dispatch a trusted envoy under cover of darkness to offer the oligarchs a sworn secret protocol: permanent recognition of their governing authority, exclusive trade rights with Athenian-aligned ports, and a binding pledge of non-interference in their internal affairs, in exchange for their public declaration of armed neutrality and explicit denial of harbor or provisioning to Spartan forces.  
Seal the pact by exchanging high-born hostages from both sides—Athenian hostages held in the city's acropolis, oligarchic children held aboard the flagship—ensuring compliance through mutual deterrence rather than siege or democratic revolution.

By smuggling cached Thessalian daggers and a cadre of Athenian drill-sergeants through the postern gate at the tannery district, we arm the democratic cell to seize the armory and acropolis in a single night, presenting Athens with a fait accompli puppet regime before Spartan hoplites can march from Miletus.

judge.note: Strategies lack realistic downstream costs; hostage logic and puppet regime timing underexamined.

theme: a CIVIL-WAR faction's temptation to invite a FOREIGN POWER inside the walls to crush its domestic rivals
problem: You lead the popular faction in a polis torn by stasis; your oligarchic rivals hold the acropolis and are secretly negotiating Spartan intervention. An Athenian fleet lies offshore, offering to crush them if you open the gates—but at the cost of a permanent garrison, tribute, and a massacre of fellow citizens. Do you invite the foreign power inside to annihilate your domestic enemies, or choose another path?
facets: The oligarchs are only days from concluding a pact with Sparta; delay may allow them to open the gates to the Spartan harmost first.
The Athenian commander demands not just access but hostages from your own families and the right to install a garrison on the acropolis indefinitely.
The demos is war-weary and could turn against whichever faction is seen as the city’s betrayer, yet a quick, savage victory might cow opposition.

angles.directive: Open the gates to the Athenians at night, guide their hoplites to the acropolis, and slaughter the oligarchic leaders to seize undisputed control—accepting the garrison and tribute as the price of survival.

Evacuate your partisans and their families aboard the Athenian ships, sail into voluntary exile, and plead your cause at Athens for a future restoration—preserving your faction’s bloodline and moral standing while deferring the reckoning.

Send a herald to the oligarchs proposing an equal division of magistracies, a mutual oath of amnesty, and a united embassy to both Athens and Sparta to declare the city’s strict neutrality—integrating the rivals into a power-sharing pact that shuts out all foreign interference.

Proclaim the city consecrated to Apollo Prostaterios, dispatch envoys to Delphi to announce a sacred truce, and invite delegates from the Amphictyony to witness a Panhellenic peace festival—reframing the stasis as an impious affront that no Greek power would dare violate by armed entry.

threads:
I send a skiff at the third watch with the prearranged torch signal to the Athenian flagship, then personally lead a squad to silently overpower the gatehouse and unbar the sally port.  
I guide the disembarked hoplites up the drainage culvert to the acropolis’s unguarded rear postern, storm the oligarchs’ night council, and execute every leader before dawn, accepting the garrison and tribute as the permanent price of survival.

I order the immediate embarkation of every partisan and their households onto the Athenian triremes under cover of darkness, sailing for voluntary exile in Piraeus before the oligarchs can seal their Spartan pact. Simultaneously, I dispatch a formal delegation to the Athenian ecclesia to secure a pledged restoration, leveraging our intact bloodline as moral capital and the certainty that a Spartan garrison will soon provoke the demos to welcome us back as liberators.

Dispatch a herald to the acropolis at dawn, bearing a written proposal for immediate rotation of all archonships between demos and oligarchs by lot, a publicly sworn amnesty for all past stasis killings, and the joint selection of five ambassadors to depart within the hour for the Athenian fleet and the Spartan harmost’s advance camp with a decree of armed neutrality backed by a unified city militia.  
The oligarchs gain half the magistracies and an escape from annihilation, while the demos gains the acropolis gate keys placed in neutral escrow during negotiations, making foreign entry impossible without both factions’ consent.

By issuing a public decree that consecrates the entire polis to Apollo Prostaterios and dispatching simultaneous heralds to Delphi and the Amphictyonic league to declare an ekecheiria for a Panhellenic peace festival, we convert the domestic stasis into a sacralized space that renders any armed incursion—whether Spartan or Athenian—an unambiguous act of asebeia against the god, forcing both external powers to stand down or risk collective sanction from the Amphictyony.

judge.note: Strategy 4 hallucinates; others ignore oligarch agency, Spartan timing.

theme: whether to keep a fragile, mistrusted PEACE TREATY or resume the war while the advantage still holds
problem: You are the Athenian general Nicias, architect of the fragile Peace of Nicias with Sparta. With Sparta’s key allies refusing to ratify the treaty and the Athenian war party growing restive, you must decide whether to keep the mistrusted peace and risk future encirclement, or strike now while your fleet and treasury give Athens the advantage.
facets: Sparta’s allies—Corinth and Boeotia—publicly reject the treaty and prepare their own forces, leaving the peace an empty shell.
Athens’ naval supremacy and restored treasury could erode if the peace holds, as the tribute-paying empire grows restless and rivals rebuild.
The war party, led by Alcibiades, paints peace as cowardice; the demos grows impatient, and political survival demands a decisive posture.

angles.directive: Launch a preemptive naval expedition to ravage the Peloponnesian coast tied to a lightning march against Boeotian forces, forcing Sparta to fight on Athenian terms before its allies unify.

Avoid fighting now; fortify Attica and the Long Walls, withdraw land forces behind them, and send only cavalry raids—forcing Sparta to exhaust itself in a siege while you wait for a diplomatic rupture to shift the blame for war onto them.

Dispatch envoys to Corinth and Boeotia with offers of reduced tribute, trade privileges, and political autonomy within the Delian League, peeling them away from Sparta and making the peace meaningful by absorbing its angry allies.

Call a pan-Hellenic congress under Athenian arbitration to settle all outstanding grievances, transforming the bilateral truce into a binding common peace—thus framing any Spartan resistance as lawlessness before all Hellas and recasting Athens as the guardian of justice rather than a rival empire.

threads: We will sail the entire war fleet around to the Laconian Gulf to incinerate Gytheum’s ship-sheds while an expeditionary column under Demosthenes marches by night through Oropus to fall upon the Boeotian levy assembling at Tanagra before the Spartan alliance can consolidate. With the Peloponnesian coast ablaze and Thebes’ mustering ground seized, we split the enemy’s line of mutual support and compel Corinth and Sparta to accept battle on the anvil of our choosing or forfeit the campaign season’s grain and timber.

I order the immediate recall of all Athenian hoplite garrisons from border forts into the Long Walls, reinforcing the masonry and completing the Phaleric sector to create an unbreakable land perimeter, while the entire treasury funds a dedicated cavalry screen to conduct nightly sorties into the Megarid and southern Boeotia, burning undefended farmsteads but withdrawing before any hoplite engagement.  
This transforms Attica into a supplied island-fortress whose walls Sparta’s phalanx cannot storm; their allies’ frustration at a static siege, combined with inevitable Boeotian or Corinthian armed provocations outside Laconia, will fracture Sparta’s coalition and hand Athens the diplomatic casus belli without our fleets ever risking a decisive battle.

I dispatch formal embassies to Corinth and Boeotia carrying a ratified offer of reduced tribute schedules, guaranteed maritime trade access, and internal self-governance under the Delian umbrella—framed not as submission but as privileged partnership—to dissolve their strategic rationale for remaining Spartan auxiliaries. This converts the treaty’s rejection into a targeted realignment by making Athens the more profitable patron, isolating Sparta through the defection of its own war-fighting backbone without firing a single trireme.

I will summon a pan-Hellenic congress at Athens, inviting every Greek city to submit its outstanding grievances to Athenian arbitration, thereby converting the hollow bilateral truce into a binding common peace. This recasts any Spartan refusal as lawlessness before all Hellas, isolates their dissident allies diplomatically, and positions Athens as the enforcer of collective justice rather than a rival empire.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume unrealistic coalition collapse; Strategy 3 underestimates Spartan counterbids.

theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of ALLIANCE AND REINFORCEMENT that may be a bluff or a trap
problem: As commander of the Athenian fleet at Samos, you receive a secret message from the exiled Alcibiades promising Persian gold and ships if you recall him and install an oligarchy—but you suspect his influence with the satrap may be a bluff or a trap to destabilize the democracy.
facets: The fleet is critically short of funds and cannot sustain operations without fresh resources.
Alcibiades is a brilliant but proven traitor whose word is worthless; the promised Persian aid may be entirely fabricated.
Accepting his terms means risking a political revolution that could split the fleet, provoke civil strife in Athens, and destroy the democratic resistance.

angles.directive: Detain the envoy, denounce the intrigue publicly, and purge all oligarchic sympathizers within the fleet to crush the threat before it festers.
Send a trusted emissary directly to Tissaphernes to verify the offer’s substance, while stalling Alcibiades with non-committal assent, keeping the fleet safely uncommitted.
Conditionally accept Alcibiades’ return as an advisor under democratic oversight, demanding that Tissaphernes deliver the first shipment of gold before any constitutional change is discussed.

Feign acceptance of the deal, but use the promised rendezvous to ambush and seize the Persian treasure ships or capture the envoys, turning the bluff into a counter-deception that acquires resources without political cost.

threads: I will detain Alcibiades’ envoy mid-message, extract every name under interrogation, then assemble the fleet to witness his public execution as I denounce the oligarchic conspiracy as a Persian-funded treason. Concurrently, I will order the marine detachments to arrest all known oligarchic sympathizers among the trierarchs and hoplites, confining them in chains aboard the flagship for summary court-martial before sunset.

Dispatch an Athenian envoy of unimpeachable loyalty—perhaps a trierarch with diplomatic experience—directly to Tissaphernes’ court to ascertain whether any Persian commitment actually exists, instructing him to report solely the satrap’s concrete terms. Meanwhile, receive Alcibiades’ overtures with calculated ambivalence, offering only procedural deference to the assembly’s future judgment while issuing no binding pledges, thereby immobilizing his faction without exposing the fleet to ideological fracture or premature political alignment.

I conditionally recall Alcibiades to Samos as a provisional advisor bound to a newly constituted democratic accountability council with full audit powers over his contacts and counsel, and I simultaneously dispatch a formal edict to Tissaphernes that no discussion of constitutional reform will even appear on the fleet assembly’s agenda until a verified shipment of one hundred talents of gold is physically lodged with our treasurers.

I will send a forged acceptance via a trusted intermediary, specifying a secluded coastal rendezvous for the "transfer" of Persian gold and ships, then deploy a concealed squadron to ambush the envoys and seize the treasure the moment they anchor. The captured funds and hostages will refill our war chest while the intercepted correspondence, made public, discredits Alcibiades as a Persian puppet without altering the Athenian constitution.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 risk mutiny; 3 underestimates Alcibiades' leverage.

theme: an oligarchic faction's bid to OVERTHROW a democracy by promising stability, money, and a foreign patron
problem: You are the elected strategos of a democratic city, and the oligarchic faction secretly offers you co-leadership of a new regime, backed by Spartan gold and mercenaries, promising stability and wealth; if you refuse, they will launch the coup within three days while the Athenian fleet is a month away. How do you respond?
facets: The oligarchs have a concrete pact with Sparta guaranteeing immediate military support and Persian gold upon their takeover, making foreign intervention a certainty.
The demos is exhausted by war and receptive to any promise of order, while the treasury is empty, leaving you unable to pay sailors or quickly arm loyal citizens.

Your personal popularity is the democracy’s chief defense, but the oligarchs’ offer includes a guarantee of your life and a seat in the new council, presenting a stark choice between survival with power and doomed resistance.

angles.directive: Arrest and execute the conspirators immediately in a lightning action, then summon the assembly to proclaim a democratic crusade, purging all suspected oligarchs and daring the Spartans to intervene before the people are armed.

Feign acceptance of the oligarchs’ offer, becoming their nominal partner while secretly dispatching a trusted messenger to Athens with a plea for an emergency fleet; play along just long enough to delay the coup until help arrives.

Accept the core of their plan but transform it into a mixed constitutional reform: invite the oligarchs into a new senate of property owners while preserving the popular assembly and jury courts, thus satisfying their desire for order without foreign masters.

Expose the Spartan plot publicly and reframe it as a pan-Hellenic betrayal, rallying citizens and even neutral neighbours to a sacred war for liberty; then march the citizen army not against the oligarchs but to pre-emptively strike the Spartan base at a nearby stronghold, turning an internal coup into a war of liberation.

threads: I will lure the oligarchic leaders to my home tonight under the pretense of accepting their offer, then have my household spearmen seize and behead them on the spot while securing their incriminating pact with Sparta. At dawn I will summon the assembly, display the corpses and the treaty as proof of treason, and force an immediate vote to execute every named conspirator, confiscate their estates to fund mass armament of the demos, and declare a democratic crusade that warns Sparta any landing will be met by a city already mobilized and burning its own fields.

I accept the oligarchs’ proposal in a witnessed private oath, then immediately send my cousin Iophon on a fishing skiff to Nauplia with sealed orders to hire a fast trireme and deliver a coded message to the Athenian boule demanding a squadron within fourteen days.

I will accept the offer in person at the oligarchs’ war council, then table a constitutional amendment before the existing assembly the next morning that creates a property-timocratic senate with binding legislative review but permanently preserves the assembly’s sole authority over war, courts, and the election of strategoi, thereby splitting their coalition by giving the merchant class a stake in the new order without triggering Spartan intervention.

I publicly produce the oligarchs' written offer and frame it as a Spartan-funded betrayal of all free Greeks, calling a sacred Panhellenic war for liberty that draws neutral neighbours into the citizen levy. I then march the assembled army not against the oligarchs' houses but to pre-emptively storm the Spartan garrison at Decelea, transforming the coup into a foreign invasion and forcing the oligarchs to either fight alongside us or be exposed as enemy collaborators.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume oligarchs won't preempt; 2 underestimates 3-day window.

theme: a besieged city's choice to SURRENDER on terms, hold out for a rescue that may never come, or break out by force
problem: As the commander of the Ionian city of Teos, besieged by the Spartan general Thibron while allied with Athens, with provisions for two months and no sign of the storm-scattered Athenian fleet, you must act: the Spartans offer safe passage and full citizenship if you renounce Athens, but starvation and factional unrest loom.
facets: The Athenian relief fleet was last seen broken by a squall off Chios and may never arrive, yet abandoning Athens invites future reprisal from the empire.
Spartan terms are unexpectedly generous—they seek a willing ally, not a sacked city—but accepting makes Teos a permanent enemy of Athens.
The populace is split: the landed class leans toward Sparta, the democrats and rowers toward Athens; with dwindling grain, the people may riot and open the gates themselves.

angles.directive: Muster every able body under arms, sally forth at moonless night against the siege-works, and fight your way to the harbour to board whatever ships remain.
Accept Thibron's terms, open the gates, and lead Teos into the Peloponnesian League, becoming a base for Spartan naval operations in Ionia.
Seal the granaries, impose severe rationing, dispatch swift messengers in small craft to plead with the Athenian navarch, and hold the walls at all costs.
Send a secret embassy to the Persian satrap Tissaphernes, offering Teos as a vassal and a chest of gold, to persuade him to fall upon the Spartans' flank with his cavalry.

threads: I will draft every male citizen, metic, and slave promised freedom into assault columns armed with shipwrights’ axes, gutting-hooks, and oil-soaked brush, then strike the Spartan circumvallation at its thinnest join—the half-built timber stretch above the marshy draw—three hours before moonset, using no trumpets but relayed hand-signals. Once through, the columns pivot hard for the harbour, overwhelm the dock-guards, commandeer every oared vessel and caïque still afloat, torch the remainder at their moorings, and make for the open sea under oars alone, steering east-by-northeast to intercept the scattered Athenian squadrons off Chios.

I order the gates opened tonight under the pretense of a grain distribution, admit Thibron’s garrison to seize the acropolis and harbor, and announce Teos’s formal secession from the Delian League at dawn. I immediately transfer our dockyards and grain reserves to Spartan control, designating Teos as the permanent forward base for the Peloponnesian fleet in Ionia while the garrison disarms the pro-Athenian rowers and executes the democratic faction’s leaders.

I order the granaries sealed and placed under armed guard, with daily grain allowances cut to a survival minimum for all inhabitants, while simultaneously launching three swift pentekonters under cover of darkness to locate the Athenian fleet and deliver a direct plea for immediate relief. The walls will be reinforced with every able body, rotating watches to prevent exhaustion, and any internal faction threatening to open the gates will be met with summary execution to preserve the defense.

Dispatch a cloaked envoy to Tissaphernes bearing a chest of gold and a signed charter of vassalage, instructing him to hurl his cavalry against Thibron’s unguarded western flank before the next moon. The gates remain barred to all until Persian lances break the siege, then Teos opens to the satrap’s garrison—not to Athens or Sparta.

judge.note: Strategies 1,3 ignore Persian power; 2,4 assume Spartan/Persian reliability.

theme: holding a newly conquered HEREDITARY state whose old ruling bloodline still has popular sympathy
problem: You have seized the throne of a hereditary state, but the old king’s young son escaped to the countryside, where he is sheltered by loyalist nobles and openly revered by the common people, threatening a prolonged insurgency.
facets: The heir’s bloodline is the sole focus of popular loyalty; eliminating him could create a martyr, while leaving him free risks rebellion.
The nobles protecting him are entrenched locally, able to wage guerrilla war from terrain they know, draining your resources over time.
The people’s passive sympathy for the old dynasty means overt brutality might ignite a general uprising, yet leniency may be seen as weakness.

angles.directive: Launch an overwhelming manhunt for the heir and his noble backers, then execute them publicly to extinguish the bloodline and terrorize all into submission.

Withdraw your main forces to the capital, fortify it, and refuse to chase the heir into the countryside; wait for the loyalist coalition to fracture from internal disputes and the people’s fervor to cool with time.
Offer the heir a marriage into your own family and a high ceremonial role, blending the bloodlines so the people transfer their affection to the new hybrid dynasty.

Unearth or fabricate evidence that the old dynasty committed hidden atrocities against the people, transforming the heir from a beloved symbol into a reviled heir of crimes, and present your conquest as liberation.

threads: Execute a decapitation strike by deploying elite cavalry and paid informants to pinpoint the heir’s location within the loyalist nobles’ estates, then stage a mass public immolation of the entire captured bloodline and their protectors in the capital square to fuse the spectacle of extinction with an irreversible collective trauma.

I withdraw every legion to the capital and seal the gates, converting the city into a self-sustaining fortress that offers no battle, while I broadcast a decree that recognizes any noble who bends the knee within six months as the rightful lord of his rival’s estates, turning their mutual suspicion into a self-devouring feud. The heir becomes a wandering claimant with no army to fight and no unified front to lead, while the peasantry’s revolutionary heat dissipates into harvest cycles and local grumbling.

I will publicly betroth the escaped prince to my eldest daughter, merging the bloodlines into a single hybrid heir, and simultaneously install him as "Royal Consort and High Steward," a permanent ceremonial office that places him at the center of all court ritual and public appearances without any military or administrative authority. This channels the people’s dynastic loyalty directly into the new joint household, transforming his symbolic presence from a rallying point for rebellion into a legitimizing ornament of my reign.

I commission a master forger to produce letters in the old king’s hand ordering the mass execution of prisoners during the Red Fever, then have a loyal priest "discover" them in the temple crypt during the heir’s birthday rites and disseminate the revelation via town criers alongside an edict that annuls all blood oaths to the dynasty and grants a three-month tax holiday to any village renouncing the heir by name. This transforms my coup into a holy liberation from a child-killing bloodline, reframing any loyalist resistance as defense of a regime of proven atrocity rather than a claimant of rightful rule.

judge.note: Strategies 3&4 assume unrealistic compliance; downstream resistance underexplored.

theme: a MIXED principality: a newly annexed territory of different language and customs
problem: You have seized a mountainous province of foreign tongue and ancient laws; the deposed prince’s heirs plot from exile across the border, local headmen defy your governors, and a watchful rival power arms the exiles. Your treasury bleeds and your garrisons are overstretched—how do you hold this mixed principality?
facets: The exiled bloodline retains a near-sacred legitimacy that makes any local rebellion appear righteous, not merely criminal.
The province’s rugged terrain and linguistic isolation make direct military occupation ruinously expensive, slow, and liable to bleed you dry.
A neighboring great power actively funds and shelters the heirs, turning even a minor internal disturbance into an immediate external war threat.

angles.directive: Launch a ruthless manhunt to capture or kill every scion of the old bloodline, even those in sanctuary; publicly execute any headman who aided them, to shatter the myth of their invulnerability and terrify the population into submission through a single, overwhelming shock.

Withdraw your garrisons from the highland valleys, abandon the hinterland to de facto local self-rule, and concentrate your forces on fortifying the coastal approaches; send envoys to the rival with false territorial concessions to buy a truce while you secretly rebuild your army for a future reconquest.

Offer the leading headmen hereditary titles in your own peerage and a share of the province’s tax revenues; let them keep their ancient laws and speak their tongue in local administration, in exchange for oaths of fealty and the delivery of the exiles into your custody, turning them into your own magistrates.

Found a new fortress-colony in the strategic heart of the province, settled entirely with your own loyal subjects; issue a new legal charter that erases all ancient privileges, and reorient the territory’s economy and identity around this city, so the province ceases to be a conquered land and becomes a fresh extension of your own realm.

threads:
Dispatch a deniable black-squad of mountaineers and poisoners across the frontier to exterminate every exiled heir, even those sheltered in temple sanctuaries, timing the strikes for the same moonless night.  
Then, at dawn, impale every headman who communicated with them on the central market square, leaving the corpses displayed with forged confessions pinned to their chests to shatter the bloodline’s sacred legend through a single, unanswerable atrocity.

We dismantle the occupation by withdrawing all garrisons from the interior valleys to a fortified coastal cordon anchored at three deepwater ports, ceding tax collection and local justice to the headmen in exchange for a formal oath of non-aggression with the rival power. Simultaneously, we dispatch envoys offering the rival a publicly humiliating but strategically empty cession of two uninhabitable border ridges as a five-year truce, while using the fiscal windfall from abandoned hinterland expenditures to triple the marine landing force in secret dockyards for a decapitation strike on the exiles’ court the moment the truce expires.

By royal patent, I will elevate each district headman to the hereditary rank of marcher lord within my own peerage, assign them a thirty percent portion of the province’s annual land tax, and confirm their right to administer justice in their customary tongue, provided they swear fealty and deliver the exiled bloodline into my custody within the year. This re-founds their authority as a direct grant from my crown, making their new wealth and status contingent on my survival and compelling them to treat the exiles as mortal enemies whose restoration would annul their patents and reclaim the revenue they now keep.

I will transplant eight thousand veteran families from the crownlands into a walled city built at the confluence of the province’s two main rivers, granting them hereditary land-tenure and tax immunities conditional on garrison service, while simultaneously promulgating a *Codex Terrae Novae* that extinguishes every customary title, clan jurisdiction, and manorial privilege, replacing them with a single legal status derived from the city’s charter. I will then re-channel all regional mining and timber revenues through the colony’s market, making the new city the sole legal entrepôt for trade with the outside world, so that within a generation the province’s economic and social gravity reorients around this implant and the old bloodline’s memory becomes irrelevant to daily life.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 assume unrealistic multi-generational success; Strategy 1 ignores blowback.

theme: holding a conquered FREE REPUBLIC long accustomed to self-rule
problem: You have just conquered a prosperous free republic that has governed itself for generations; its citizens despise foreign rule and its institutions are intact, making complete destruction as ruinous as leniency is dangerous.
facets: The population’s identity is fused with self-governance, so any ruler is seen as a tyrant regardless of benefits provided.
The existing civic institutions (assemblies, councils, laws) are engines of collective memory and can rapidly organize resistance if not dealt with.
The city’s wealth depends on its merchant and artisan classes, who will flee or sabotage prosperity if terrorized, yet are the very source of republican sentiment.

angles.directive: Raze the city entirely, scatter its population, and erase its name from maps—so that no memory of the republic can ever inspire rebellion in this or neighboring territories.

Preserve the outward forms of the republic—assemblies, titles, elections—but systematically ensure all candidates and officials are your clients, transforming self-rule into a theater that legitimizes your control while exhausting dissent through managed participation.

Install a native puppet ruler from a rival faction within the republic, funding and arming their regime while you withdraw to a nearby fortress; let the puppet absorb the hatred of restoring order, then return as liberator when the city exhausts itself.

Found an entirely new city on a different site, transfer all commerce and privileges there under a fresh identity and constitution you author, then let the old republic wither as a provincial backwater deprived of its economic and elite class.

threads: Raze the city’s physical grid to its foundations, execute or deport the entire population beyond the watershed, and strike the settlement’s name from all imperial records and monuments so that future maps show only uninhabited terrain at those coordinates.

I will issue an edict retaining every assembly and elective office, but I will establish a mandatory loyalty oath administered by my personal secretariat as a prerequisite for candidacy, while simultaneously creating a state grain fund that extends credit exclusively to merchants who publicly endorse my nominated candidates, turning every election into a managed contest among indebted clients.

Install a prominent merchant-guild exile as titular governor with a mercenary garrison funded by a direct tax on the city’s own trade revenues, then withdraw your main force to a fortified citadel twenty miles distant. Let the puppet’s tax collectors and hired swords fracture the republican institutions from within while you wait for the exhausted citizenry to beg for your intervention against the regime you creado.

I will construct an entirely new city at a strategically chosen site and grant it a charter that mimics civic assemblies under a puppet senate appointed by my administration, thereby creating a controlled outlet for republican impulses. I will then compel the relocation of the old city’s entire merchant and artisan elite by issuing exclusive trading rights and tax immunities to those who resettle within one year, while revoking the old city’s commercial privileges, dissolving its councils, and reducing it to a rural market town without administrative status.

judge.note: Strategy 3 risks uncontrolled collapse; Strategy 4 assumes merchant mobility.

theme: whether to be FEARED or LOVED by newly acquired subjects
problem: A prince has annexed a fiercely independent city-state whose citizens despise foreign rule; he must choose whether to pacify them through fear or cultivate their loyalty through love, knowing that a half-measure will invite insurrection.
facets: The city's economy relies on a network of guilds that could paralyze commerce if alienated, yet also serve as natural surveillance nodes if co-opted.
A deposed heir from the former ruling house has escaped to a rival power and agitates for liberation, making every disaffected citizen a potential fifth columnist.
The prince's own army is overextended and weary from the conquest, limiting his capacity to sustain a prolonged occupation or brutal crackdown.

angles.directive: Decimate the guild leadership and execute any citizen suspected of communicating with the deposed heir, making the cost of resistance so monstrously high that obedience becomes instinctual, while requisitioning the guilds' wealth to pay mercenary reinforcements.

Restore the guilds' traditional privileges and appoint their most influential masters to the new ruling council, binding their prosperity to your regime's stability while publicly betrothing your daughter to a respected local family to symbolize a shared future.

Withdraw the bulk of your troops to a fortified garrison outside the walls, grant the city formal autonomy under a puppet council of elders, and wait for factional greed or the heir's rash return to create a crisis that forces the citizens to beg for your intervention on your terms.

Announce a grand public works project—a new cathedral and market square designed by the city's own artisans—funded by a temporary tax exemption, and craft a civic cult around a local saint who allegedly prophesied a 'righteous foreign guardian,' thus recasting the occupation as a sacred destiny rather than a subjugation.

threads: I will publicly behead the entire guild council in the main square, confiscate their treasuries, and use the seized gold to immediately contract a company of sellswords to replace my weary garrison. I will then issue a proclamation that any citizen caught receiving or transmitting messages from the deposed heir will be burned alive inside their guildhall, transforming every guild into a collective hostage that polices its own members or perishes together.

By immediately reaffirming the guild charters and installing their Grand Masters as a permanent majority bloc on a new Council of Twelve with binding veto over taxation, we fuse the city’s economic bloodstream to our regime’s pulse. The public betrothal of Princess Alina to the Clarimond heir then converts lineage into leverage, rendering any insurrection a self-inflicted wound on the merchant elite’s own flesh and future.

Pull the army back to a fortified camp beyond the walls while installing a council of guild elders as nominal rulers, covertly funding rival factions within each guild to ensure paralysis unless they petition for your arbitration. When the exiled heir's return or internal bidding war triggers a breakdown of order, the city's own merchant-elite will deliver the keys and a formal request for direct rule under terms you dictate.

The prince commissions the city’s guild masters to design and build a cathedral and market square, funded by a temporary exemption on guild taxes, binding their economic survival to the regime’s stability and turning their networks into self-policing surveillance nodes. He simultaneously fabricates a civic cult around a local saint’s obscure prophecy of a “righteous foreign guardian,” staging guild-led processions and feast days that recast the occupation as sacred destiny, thereby making the exiled heir’s liberation rhetoric sound like blasphemy against the city’s own divine plan.

judge.note: Strategies 3&4 assume elite compliance; underestimate spontaneous resistance.

theme: the TIMING AND DOSAGE OF CRUELTY — all at once vs spread out — to pacify a disorderly new state
problem: You have just seized a fractious city-state, and its noble families are already conspiring. You must pacify the state—how do you approach the timing and dosage of cruelty?
facets: Your mercenary army will dissolve without immediate plunder.
The city's merchants will flee if instability persists, collapsing trade.
Your foreign patron demands a stable regime within days or will withdraw all support.

angles.directive: Strike all at once: arrest and execute every suspected conspirator and their male heirs in a single night, then immediately issue a general pardon, reduce taxes, and reward the commoners with confiscated property.
Abandon the city to the conspirators, withdraw to a fortified stronghold, and wait for the nobles to exhaust themselves in internecine warfare before returning to reclaim a weakened state.

Invite the leading conspirators to form a new governing council, grant them lucrative tax-farming concessions, and betroth your heir to the most powerful family, intertwining their fate with your own survival.

Arm the city's disenfranchised guilds, declare a popular republic, and convene a revolutionary tribunal to try the nobles under new laws, transforming needed violence into an act of public justice rather than princely cruelty.

threads: Order the Night of the Unstrung Bow: at the third watch, mercenary companies simultaneously breach the compounds of all seventeen suspect noble families to execute every male over the age of twelve, then by dawn redistribute the seized estates in fee simple to the city’s artisan guilds while announcing a permanent halving of the port tax.

I will strip the city’s treasury and granaries to give the mercenaries their plunder in advance, then withdraw the entire army to the Karad citadel and issue a decree that I abandon the city to the noble houses, sealing the gates behind me. When the nobles tear each other apart over the empty shell and merchant flight starves their factions, I will return with my intact force to crush the exhausted survivors and install a permanent garrison under my patron’s mandate.

We offer the seven leading conspirators immediate seats on a Regency Council with apportioned tax-farming rights over the city’s customs houses, then seal the arrangement by publicly betrothing our heir to the Velaran patriarch’s eldest daughter, converting their coup calculus into a balance-sheet problem where our collapse would annihilate their newly vested revenue streams and dynastic standing simultaneously.

Arm every disenfranchised guild — smiths, tanners, stevedores — from the noble armories, declare the Guild Republic of the city, and empanel a Revolutionary Tribunal to sentence the conspiring families to death and estate forfeiture under a newly promulgated Law of Public Safety. This channels the unavoidable violence into a judicial terror that grants the mercenaries their loot through organized confiscation, prevents merchant flight by substituting law for massacre, and gives our patron a stable regime before the ultimatum expires.

judge.note: Strategy 2 assumes predictable noble self-destruction; Strategy 4 risks mercenary/guild friction.

theme: whether to KEEP or BREAK a promise/treaty now that the circumstances that justified it have changed
problem: You promised a rival condottiero a strategic city in return for military aid to crush a rebellion; now the province is pacified, but he is secretly arming your deposed nobles, and his mercenary company has swollen beyond what you can easily match. The treaty was struck when the condottiero seemed too weak to threaten you—those conditions have now reversed.
facets: The condottiero's covert support for your internal enemies gives you a legitimate casus belli, but exposing it might precipitate the war you are not yet prepared to win.
Your reputation for honoring oaths is the bedrock of your alliances; a sudden breach without ironclad proof would make other allies view you as faithless.
The city’s fortifications command the mountain pass—handing it over would leave your capital exposed if the condottiero later moves against you.

angles.directive: Publicly denounce the condottiero's treachery, stage a preemptive assault on his encampment, and seize the city you promised, breaking the treaty openly while the momentum of surprise is yours.

Honor the treaty in word but not in deed: dispatch endless envoys to negotiate boundaries, inventories, and transfer protocols, delaying the handover indefinitely while you secretly reinforce your frontier and seek foreign backing.

Summon the condottiero to a grand reconciliation council, offer him your daughter in marriage and the title of Grand Constable of all your forces, merging his ambitions with your dynasty and making the promised city a moot point within a larger union.

Convene a learned commission of canon and civil jurists to pronounce the treaty void ab initio on grounds of the condottiero's silent breach of good faith, transforming the act of withholding the city from a broken promise into a vindication of princely honor.

threads: Dispatch heralds at midnight with forged correspondence proving the condottiero’s payments to the exiled nobles, then strike his main camp at the hour of changing watch with massed crossbowmen and dismounted men-at-arms to overrun the sleeping mercenaries before they can form a line. Simultaneously, a picked company disguised as a supply convoy enters the promised city’s gatehouse, seizes the winch room, and bars the inner ward against the condottiero’s garrison while your main body occupies the outer fortifications from the mountain side.

Idispatch a permanent legation of jurists, surveyors, and quartermasters to the condottiero with a remit to re-measure every boundary stone, audit every grain in the city’s stores, and secure notarized releases from every distant suzerain with a theoretical claim, transforming the handover into an infinite procedural labyrinth that never technically breaches the treaty. Simultaneously, I divert the salt tax to subsidize the Alpine cantons’ militias in exchange for a secret defensive pact and begin quietly stockpiling saltpeter and seasoned timber in the mountain forts under the cover of “routine bridge inspections.”

Summon the condottiero to the capital for a public betrothal ceremony and simultaneously issue sealed patents naming him Grand Constable with authority over all state forces—including the city in question, now reclassified as his official military governorship under your supreme command—so that the marriage merges his bloodline with your succession and converts his independent company into the realm’s standing army under your dynastic banner.

I empanel a commission of the realm’s most authoritative doctors of canon and civil law to examine the condottiero’s covert arming of deposed nobles and issue a formal sentence pronouncing the treaty void *ab initio* for *dolus ex silentio*—fraudulent concealment that fatally poisoned the agreement’s good faith at signing—which retroactively transforms my refusal to hand over the city from a promise broken into a vindication of princely rectitude. I then have that sentence read publicly in every allied chancery and cathedral and invite neutral envoys to inspect a curated dossier of his intercepted communications, forcing him into the posture of either swallowing the legal defeat or openly defying a verdict of honourable scholars, which would shatter his own alliances and morally isolate him before any shot is fired.

judge.note: Strategy 3 underestimates resistance to forced integration; Strategy 4 assumes legal authority suffices.

theme: MERCENARIES vs CITIZEN-ARMS vs auxiliaries for the state's defense
problem: A newly installed prince must defend his restive city-state from imminent invasion, but the mercenary captains who helped him seize it now demand extortionate pay and threaten defection, while accepting auxiliaries from a powerful neighboring king would reduce him to a client, and arming his own disloyal citizens risks rebellion before the enemy even arrives.
facets: Mercenary loyalty is purely transactional and vanishes when pay falters or a higher bidder appears.
Auxiliaries from a stronger power fight for their own master's interests, making the prince a permanent vassal.
Disarmed and resentful citizens cannot be turned into reliable soldiers overnight without first earning their trust.

angles.directive: Massacre the treacherous mercenary captains, seize their accumulated wealth, and use it to immediately hire more disciplined replacements while publicly executing any who waver, enforcing obedience through terror.

Pretend to negotiate the auxiliaries' entry while stalling with diplomatic technicalities; covertly train a citizen militia in secret, then publicly reject the foreign troops once your own force is viable, daring the neighbor to attack a united, armed populace.

Grant the mercenary captains noble titles, hereditary lands, and a stake in the city's survival by marrying them into local families, transforming them from paid adventurers into a loyal military aristocracy bound to the state's fate.

Refuse conventional field armies; instead, fortify the city, arm only a small trusted corps, and distribute propaganda that frames the coming invasion as a war of liberation, aiming to bog the enemy down in attritional urban combat while rallying the populace around a defensive myth.

threads: To break both the mercenaries’ extortion and the citizenry’s rebellious inertia with a single stroke, we stage a banquet for the captains, poison their wine, and publicly display their corpses as evidence of treachery against the state, then conscript their hoarded silver to commission a fresh, smaller-but-loyal household guard recruited from the neighboring king’s unemployed drillmasters rather than his soldiers.

I will stall the neighboring king’s auxiliary offer by having my envoys haggle interminably over sacred ritual observances and dynastic guarantees in the treaty’s preamble, rendering the pact unconsummated for months. Meanwhile, I will drain the city armories in secret nightly shifts, cycling disarmed citizens through weapon drill in underground cisterns and purging likely collaborators, then publicly tear up the unsigned foreign pact the moment they can execute a basic phalanx wheel, daring the neighbor to assault a polity now defined by armed popular unity.

Grant the mercenary captains hereditary fiefs carved from the estates of the prince’s defeated internal rivals and seal each grant with a political marriage to a daughter of the old noble houses, transforming their transactional loyalty into a legally entrenched, land-bound military aristocracy whose fortunes now rise or fall with the city’s survival.

He seals the gates, stockpiles grain, and deploys a handpicked palace guard to hold the citadel while flooding the streets with leaflets declaring the enemy will disarm every citizen and enslave their children, transforming the siege into a holy defense of hearth and home. The prince broadcasts daily from the ramparts that the invaders are the old tyrant's foreign pawns come to reclaim their chains, goading the populace to booby-trap alleyways and melt into the warren of tenements so that every block extracted costs the enemy a battalion in bleeding, demoralizing house-to-house attrition.

judge.note: Strategies 2 & 4 assume populace loyalty without addressing why disloyal citizens suddenly unite.

theme: rewarding the OVER-MIGHTY NOBLE whose aid won the throne but who now rivals the prince
problem: You seized the crown with the sword of Duke Valerio, who now garrisons your capital's gates, demands the title of Co-Prince and a veto over every decree, while his household troops mock your authority. Refusing may bring his veterans into your throne room; yielding makes you a puppet. How do you secure your rule?
facets: The Duke's personal guard and mercenary captains outnumber your loyal household troops three to one, and they hold the city's main fortifications.
He commands deep loyalty among the lesser nobility and the soldiery, who see him as the true victor, while foreign powers watch for a pretext to invade a divided realm.
Any open move against him risks immediate civil war, but allowing him to accumulate more offices and land will soon make him impossible to dislodge without total ruin.

angles.directive: Invite the Duke to a private victory banquet in the palace's inner hall, have your most trusted guards seize and execute him during the toast, then announce that he was plotting with a foreign crown—immediately sending sealed orders to loyal garrisons to arrest his captains on similar charges before they can coordinate.

Publicly embrace his demands with grand ceremony, granting him the empty title of Co-Prince and a veto, but immediately dispatch him as supreme commander to a distant, grueling campaign against the mountain rebels, supplying him just enough to fight but not enough to win quickly, while you quietly recruit and train a new palace army in his absence.

Offer the Duke a permanent and prestigious office such as 'Lord High Steward of the Realm', with vast tax-free estates, a seat on a newly formed Council of State that you pack with your own loyalists, and a marriage between his daughter and your heir, making his family's future inseparable from the dynasty's survival.

Proclaim a foundational reform: the old autocracy is dead; in its place, create a 'Balanced Crown' where the Duke becomes ceremonial Co-Sovereign but real authority is vested in a Senate of nobles, clergy, and guildmasters that you can dominate through patronage—turning the Duke's power into a symbolic office while you control the treasury and the new bureaucracy.

threads: I will host a private victory banquet in the palace’s inner hall, and as I raise my goblet in a toast to Duke Valerio, my hand-picked household guards—concealed behind the arras—will step forward and cut him down before he lowers his own cup. The moment the body falls, I will proclaim to the assembled court that he was caught in a treasonable conspiracy with a foreign power, and dispatch sealed couriers to every loyal garrison with pre-drafted orders to arrest his mercenary captains under the same charge before they can rally.

I will publicly invest Duke Valerio as Co-Prince with full ceremonial honors and a formal decree granting him an irrevocable legislative veto, then immediately dispatch him as Supreme Commander to crush the mountain rebels with a commission that shackles his veteran cohorts to a lean supply train calibrated for stalemate, not victory. In the vacuum he leaves behind, I will raise a new palace army through a covert levy on the capital’s guilds, drilling them inside the inner citadel under officers handpicked from my household who have no ties to the old nobility.

I will invest Duke Valerio with the hereditary office of Lord High Steward, granting him tax-free estates that revert to the crown should his line fail to produce a male heir by my daughter, while simultaneously seating him on a packed Council of State where my loyalists outnumber his faction three to two, and I will betroth his only daughter to my son the same hour, so that any move against me annihilates his own blood’s claim to immortality.

I proclaim the monarchy transformed: Duke Valerio is crowned as ceremonial Co-Sovereign without authority over decree or treasury, while I instantly constitute a Senate of 300 lesser nobles, bishops, and guildmasters whose seats I endow personally from crown lands and whose voting blocs I bind through the royal purse and newly created bureaucratic offices. Simultaneously, I appoint every tax collector, mint warden, and chancery clerk directly under my seal, converting the Duke’s armed garrison into a hollow spectacle that cannot spend, legislate, or administer without the Senate’s patronage strings I alone pull.

judge.note: Strategies 1&2 assume flawless execution; 3&4 underestimate noble faction backlash.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: After recapturing the rebel city of Mytilene, the Athenian Assembly must decide its fate, knowing that a harsh punishment would terrify other subject-allies into obedience but risk hardening their hatred, while a lenient course might encourage further revolts across the empire.
facets: The deterrent effect on current and future allies, and whether fear or loyalty is the more reliable bond.
The risk of creating a cycle of perpetual rebellion by making surrender unthinkable for future insurgents.
The deep domestic factionalism within the Assembly, with hawks demanding blood and doves advocating restraint.

angles.directive: Annihilate Mytilene: execute all adult males, enslave the women and children, and raze the city. Let every island ally behold the smoke as the immutable price of defiance.

Defer judgment by dispatching a commission to investigate the causes of the revolt and negotiate with influential locals, while reinforcing garrisons in other cities. Publicly, declare that justice will follow careful deliberation, buying time for tempers to cool and alliances to stabilize.

Spare the city but execute only the oligarchic ringleaders who led the revolt. Install a democratic constitution loyal to Athens, grant amnesty to the common citizens, and allow them to retain their lands, turning a subdued populace into a dependent ally.

Pardon Mytilene entirely and proclaim a new imperial charter that reduces tribute obligations and grants greater local autonomy to all subject-allies. Reframe the empire as a voluntary league against external threats, arguing that satisfied partners serve more steadily than coerced subjects.

threads: The Athenian Assembly dispatches a fleet to Mytilene with orders to execute every adult male, enslave the remaining population, and systematically dismantle the city’s structures until only the blackened foundations remain visible from the sea lanes. Every allied island that witnesses this permanent scar on the horizon will internalize that revolt triggers not punitive negotiation but unconditional extinction, converting the entire empire’s compliance from a calculation of risk into a fixed reflex of self-preservation.

They dispatch a standing investigative commission to Mytilene with a remit to audit the revolt’s local sponsors and negotiate provisional tribute terms directly with merchant factions, while simultaneously rotating existing garrison forces from Samos and Byzantium into key allied ports under the stated rationale of “fleet resupply.” The Assembly then tables the punishment vote for one Athenian month, framing the delay publicly as a procedural necessity to align the decree with the commission’s findings, thereby forcing both hawk and dove factions to bid for the commission’s leverage.

Execute solely the oligarchic ringleaders, immediately install a democratic constitution staffed by previously disenfranchised pro-Athenian elements, and issue a general amnesty that guarantees common citizens full retention of their lands and legal standing. This surgically decapitates the revolt’s leadership while converting the demos into a dependent client body whose security now hinges on Athenian backing against any oligarchic resurgence.

The Assembly votes to pardon Mytilene unconditionally while simultaneously issuing a league-wide decree that slashes all tribute obligations by a fixed quarter and transfers judicial and fiscal authority to local allied councils, thereby converting the Delian League from a tribute-extraction empire into a voluntary mutual-defense compact against Persia. This structural redefinition eliminates the insurgency's strategic calculus by making continued participation cheaper than revolt, and it dissolves the domestic hawk faction’s political leverage by removing the centralized tribute streams that fund their punitive campaigns.

judge.note: Strategy 4 fantasizes structural empire collapse; 1 oversells reflex compliance.

theme: FORTUNE vs CAUTION: whether to seize a sudden, fickle opportunity to expand
problem: A wealthy port city has expelled its tyrant and a merchant faction offers you lordship, but a powerful neighboring empire considers it a vassal and winter storms will close the sea in three weeks, isolating any expedition from reinforcement or retreat.
facets: The city controls a choke point for eastern trade, enriching whoever collects its tariffs.
The neighboring empire has long claimed suzerainty and fields a large standing army, though it is currently distracted on a distant frontier.
Winter storms will close the sea lanes in three weeks, making sea-borne supply or withdrawal impossible for months.

angles.directive: March immediately with your army, seize the citadel, execute any rival claimants, and install a loyal governor, daring the empire to respond while it is distracted.
Decline the offer, send gifts to the city's new council to preserve goodwill, and reinforce your own border forts, allowing the empire and internal factions to exhaust each other first.

Dispatch envoys and treasure to win over the city's guilds and leading families, forging a perpetual alliance that grants you tribute and military access while leaving them nominal autonomy, turning them into a willing client state.

Convene a regional congress of neighboring city-states to guarantee the port's free status and neutrality under a collective tariff, transforming your claim into a shared shield against imperial expansion and positioning yourself as the indispensable arbiter.

threads: Dispatch a vanguard of 800 heavy infantry by sea within the week, storm the citadel at night during the merchant faction’s victory banquet, and summarily execute the entire council along with any known imperial envoys, turning their invitation into a decapitation strike. Install a military governor the next morning with orders to fortify the harbor choke point and fund a permanent garrison exclusively from the eastern trade tariffs, leaving the distracted empire with only a closed winter land route and a fait accompli it cannot overturn without a full campaign season.

Decline the merchant faction’s lordship offer via sealed courier while dispatching a tribute embassy of silk, silver, and salt to the new city council to purchase an immediate non-aggression pact. Simultaneously, issue orders to reinforce all border fortifications with full winter garrison rotations, pre-positioned grain and munitions, and strict scouting protocols, letting the empire and internal factions grind each other down until the sea opens again.

Dispatch a handpicked delegation with twelve chests of bullion and a draft charter to the city’s Spice Guild and the seven largest trading houses, offering immediate payoff of their imperial debts in exchange for ratified signatures on a permanent protectorate treaty that grants us basing rights and 15% tariff share while leaving their council formal sovereignty. Have the envoys stage a public burning of the old tribute ledger in the central market and issue newly minted “alliance florins” to every registered burgher, so that by the time the empire moves, the entire city has a financial stake in repelling any reclamation force as an assault on their own property.

I will immediately dispatch envoys to the five neighboring maritime city-states proposing an emergency congress to establish the port as a permanently neutral free city under a collective tariff-sharing pact, with a rotating council of city-state representatives and myself as the neutral executive arbiter. This transforms my claim from a unilateral lordship into a multilateral shield: any imperial attack on the port triggers a mutual defense clause that forces the empire to fight a coalition on multiple fronts, while the shared tariff revenue incentivizes the city-states to uphold the arrangement through the winter isolation.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume coalition/empire passivity; winter isolation underexplored.

theme: holding a newly conquered HEREDITARY state whose old ruling bloodline still has popular sympathy
problem: You have seized a hereditary duchy whose prince died without an heir in your camp, yet his young nephew survives in exile and the populace quietly venerates the old bloodline as the sole legitimate source of rule. You must secure the province before the nephew matures into a rallying point.
facets: The old prince was beloved and his death in your custody is whispered to be betrayal, not fate.
The exiled nephew is currently a child under distant foreign protection, harmless now but a future beacon for rebellion.
The local nobles publicly kneel yet privately fund shrines to the deposed dynasty, testing where power truly lies.

angles.directive: Dispatch agents to assassinate the exiled nephew immediately, extinguishing the bloodline so no future claimant can crystallize resistance, while simultaneously purging any nobles who dare mourn the old prince.

Betroth your own daughter to the young nephew, declaring him your heir to the duchy under a new combined dynasty, thereby grafting his legitimacy onto your rule while raising him in your own court as a controlled ward.

Declare the old prince a tyrant posthumously through forged evidence of intended mass executions, then institute radical land redistribution to the peasantry, recasting yourself as liberator who broke a corrupt bloodline so thoroughly that restoration becomes undesirable.

Withdraw your occupying garrison entirely and install local magistrates chosen by the people, granting the province formal autonomy under your distant suzerainty, accepting that you lose direct control and tax revenue but deny the nephew any oppression to campaign against.

threads: Send a three-man team of poisoners disguised as itinerant merchants to the foreign court where the nephew resides, and have them slip a slow-acting, symptom-mimicking toxin into his evening milk so that his death appears a natural childhood fever within a fortnight. Simultaneously, compile a list of every noble household that has funded a shrine or publicly displayed mourning colors, then deploy the ducal guard to arrest them for sedition at dawn, seizing their estates and executing the heads of each family before noon to collapse any nascent resistance network.

Betroth your daughter to the exiled nephew immediately, declaring him co-sovereign under a fused dynastic name, then physically transfer him from his foreign guardians into your court as a permanent ward whose education, marriage, and daily retinue you control until majority.

I forge a secret decree bearing the old prince’s seal that orders the mass execution of every village headman as suspected conspirators, then publicly proclaim this “uncovered” tyranny to justify confiscating all noble estates and distributing their lands directly to the peasants in hereditary freehold, irreversibly tying their material survival to my new order and making any restoration of the old bloodline an existential threat to their newfound property.

I dissolve the ducal garrison and charter a Council of Aldermen elected by freeholders and guild masters, vesting them with full fiscal and judicial authority while remitting all taxes bar a single annual silver rose as token of suzerainty. By making the province self-governing and prosperity its own garrison, I render any future invasion by the nephew a foreign conquest rather than a liberation, starving his blood-claim of the grievance it needs to ignite.

judge.note: Strategies 1&3 assume flawless execution; 4 underestimates blood-claim resilience.

theme: whether to KEEP or BREAK a promise/treaty now that the circumstances that justified it have changed
problem: You promised the conquered city's council immunity and self-rule in exchange for their bloodless surrender; now, with rebellion stirring in neighboring provinces, that autonomy lets them fund dissent and withhold grain.
facets: The promise was the cornerstone of your reputation for leniency, which encourages future surrenders without siege.
The city's grain reserves and treasury are critical to supply your army for the coming campaign season.
The council is legally in compliance—their dissent is covert, through merchants and proxies, giving you no public breach to justify action.

angles.directive: Offer the council seats in your own senate and marriage ties that bind their leading families to your house, converting them from autonomous rivals into invested co-governors of the larger realm, so their grain and gold flow voluntarily to a shared cause.

Engineer a provocation—a forged assassination plot or a staged border skirmish—to manufacture a public breach of loyalty, then sweep in with overwhelming force to dissolve the council, seize the granaries, and make the city a terrifying example that cowes neighboring provinces into submission.
